_meta.description: Ransomware IR Toolbox — Containment & Eradication Actions
_meta.notes: Edit this file to add, remove, or update containment actions. Each action needs a unique id (integer). Phases: initial_access | lateral_movement | pre_encryption | active_encryption | post_encryption. Infra: windows_ad | vmware | cloud | saas | federated | mixed. TA tags: rdp | rmm | exfil | cred_dump | dc_access | backup_access | unknown. Killchain: recon | initial_access | execution | persistence | privilege_esc | lateral | collection | impact. Effectiveness: High | Medium-High | Medium | Low-Medium | Low. Business_impact: High | Medium-High | Medium | Low-Medium | Low. CES (Containment Effectiveness Score): integer 1–4.   1=LOW: low effectiveness or low-value activity.   2=MODERATE: medium effectiveness or high-impact action.   3=GOOD: high effectiveness with moderate impact, or essential emergency actions.   4=OPTIMAL: high effectiveness with low disruption — prioritise these first. If 'ces' is omitted, the app auto-computes it from effectiveness and business_impact. To change a score: edit the 'ces' value on the action and reload the file.

ransomware_groups.name: Unknown / Not Listed
ransomware_groups.desc: Use generic prioritization — no group-specific TTP boosts applied.

ransomware_groups.name: LockBit 3.0
ransomware_groups.desc: Most prolific ransomware group. Ransomware-as-a-Service with wide affiliate network. Uses AnyDesk, Cobalt Strike, and stolen AD credentials. Targets ESXi, backups, and file servers. Fast encryption speed.

ransomware_groups.name: ALPHV / BlackCat
ransomware_groups.desc: Sophisticated RaaS written in Rust. Uses triple extortion. Targets VMware ESXi aggressively. Known for healthcare sector attacks and working with Scattered Spider. Uses Exmatter for exfiltration.

ransomware_groups.name: Akira
ransomware_groups.desc: Emerged 2023, targets SMBs aggressively. Initial access via Cisco VPN (no MFA), AnyDesk, and compromised credentials. Exfiltrates before encryption using WinSCP and cloud storage. Known for fast dwell time.

ransomware_groups.name: Play
ransomware_groups.desc: Targets public-facing applications (FortiOS, Exchange ProxyNotShell). No affiliate model — operates privately. Disables AV/EDR using GMER and IObit Unlocker. Uses WinSCP for exfil. Known for double extortion.

ransomware_groups.name: Cl0p
ransomware_groups.desc: Known for mass exploitation of zero-days (MOVEit, GoAnywhere, Accellion). Focuses on data theft and extortion — sometimes without encryption. Long dwell time. Very large-scale operations.

ransomware_groups.name: Black Basta
ransomware_groups.desc: Former Conti members. Uses Qakbot, SystemBC, and Cobalt Strike. Targets ESXi and Windows simultaneously. Fast encryption with AES-256 + RSA-4096. Uses AnyDesk and tools from Conti playbook.

ransomware_groups.name: Medusa
ransomware_groups.desc: Active since 2021, surged in 2023. Exploits RDP and phishing for initial access. Uses Cobalt Strike and Living-off-the-Land binaries. Targets backups aggressively. Double extortion via Medusa Blog.

ransomware_groups.name: Rhysida
ransomware_groups.desc: Relatively new group that surged in 2023-24. Targets healthcare aggressively. Uses PhishingRedirect, valid accounts via RDP, and Cobalt Strike. Sells victim data on leak site. Known for targeting NAS and backup systems.

ransomware_groups.name: Royal / BlackSuit
ransomware_groups.desc: Former Conti members. Uses callback phishing (BazarCall), Cobalt Strike, and legitimate admin tools. Partial encryption to evade detection. Known for targeted, manual attack style with long dwell time.

ransomware_groups.name: Scattered Spider
ransomware_groups.desc: Highly skilled social engineering group. SIM swapping, MFA fatigue, and help desk impersonation for initial access. Targets Okta, Azure AD, and cloud infrastructure. Partners with ALPHV/BlackCat for ransomware deployment.

ransomware_groups.name: Qilin
ransomware_groups.notes: Cross-platform ransomware targeting ESXi and Linux. Known for VMware-focused attacks and credential harvesting from browser stores.

ransomware_groups.name: INC Ransom
ransomware_groups.notes: Active since mid-2023. Initial access via Citrix and exposed RDP. Deploys encryptors across Windows and Linux systems.

ransomware_groups.name: Warlock
ransomware_groups.notes: Emerging ransomware group. Phishing-led initial access followed by credential harvesting and data theft before encryption.

ransomware_groups.name: Lynx
ransomware_groups.notes: Active group targeting mid-market organisations. Leverages valid credentials and RDP for lateral movement.

ransomware_groups.name: DragonForce
ransomware_groups.notes: Ransomware-as-a-service operator. Overlaps with Scattered Spider TTPs — social engineering, vishing, SaaS targeting. Claimed responsibility for UK retail attacks in 2025.

ransomware_groups.name: SafePay
ransomware_groups.notes: Emerging group targeting critical infrastructure and enterprise environments. VMware and Windows environments primary focus.

ransomware_groups.name: Sinobi
ransomware_groups.notes: Emerging ransomware group. Limited public reporting. Phishing-led with standard credential harvesting and lateral movement pattern.

actions.notes:
Isolate all systems considered critical to operations when the full scope of attacker access is unknown and there is insufficient visibility to apply more targeted containment. Use this action when you cannot yet determine which specific hosts are compromised and need to reduce the blast radius immediately.

Systems to prioritise for isolation:
• Domain Controllers and authentication infrastructure
• Backup servers and storage systems
• File servers holding sensitive or business-critical data
• Financial and ERP systems
• Any system whose compromise would enable ransomware to spread further or survive recovery

Isolation methods: use EDR network isolation if agents are deployed and active, VLAN reassignment at the switch, firewall ACL per host IP, or physical cable pull for on-premises systems. Do not power off isolated systems — volatile memory (running processes, active connections, encryption keys) is critical for forensics.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 4 (Isolate Host) for targeted single-host isolation with full method guidance. ID 3 (Isolate Network) for segment-level isolation when multiple hosts in a zone are affected.

Isolate one Domain Controller per domain as a clean reference point and to protect the most sensitive AD state from further attacker manipulation. Use this action when the full scope of attacker access is unknown and there is insufficient visibility to apply more targeted containment — preserving a clean DC ensures you retain a trusted authentication and GPO baseline regardless of what else is compromised.

Prioritise the PDC Emulator first, it holds the most operationally critical FSMO roles (time synchronisation, password changes, account lockout processing, and GPO editing authority) and is the primary target for attackers seeking to maintain domain-wide control.

Before isolating, confirm the following:
• You know the DSRM (Directory Services Restore Mode) password for the DC being isolated. Without it, you cannot log into the DC in recovery mode if AD services fail.
• Offline backups of SYSVOL (%SYSTEMROOT%\SYSVOL\*) and all GPOs are available and current on the DC being isolated.
• At least one other DC remains reachable to service authentication requests during isolation, isolating the only DC in the domain will take down all domain authentication.

To identify the PDC Emulator, use the netdom query fsmo command or check the PDC Emulator attribute in Active Directory Users and Computers.

Isolation steps:
1. Disconnect all network interfaces on the DC (physical cable pull, vSwitch disconnect, or EDR network isolation, do not power off)
2. Confirm the DC is no longer reachable from the network before proceeding
3. Do not shut down or reboot: keep the DC powered on to preserve volatile memory and prevent any restart-triggered processes
4. Repeat for each domain within managed and trusted forests if the compromise scope extends across forest trusts

WARNING: If the PDC Emulator is confirmed compromised, transfer the FSMO roles to a clean DC before isolating it, otherwise PDC-dependent functions (password changes, lockout processing, time sync) will fail across the domain until roles are seized or transferred.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: This action is pinned as a Priority Action when lateral movement is confirmed and DC access has been observed. It works alongside ID 5 (Disable AD-Entra Sync) and ID 13 (Rotate All Privileged Credentials), isolate the DC and sever sync before rotating credentials to prevent new passwords from being read or overwritten via still-active replication channels.

Segment or cut off network zones where attacker activity is confirmed or suspected. Unlike host isolation (ID 4), network isolation targets an entire segment, a VLAN, subnet, or cloud VPC, and is appropriate when multiple hosts in a zone are affected or when host-by-host isolation is too slow.

Common isolation methods:
• VLAN: reassign switch ports to an isolated VLAN with no routing to production
• Firewall ACL: apply deny-all rules to the segment at the perimeter or core switch
• Cloud security group: remove inbound/outbound rules or apply a deny-all policy to the VPC or subnet
• Physical disconnect: for small environments, physically removing the uplink switch cable isolates the segment immediately

WARNING: Confirm which critical systems reside in the segment before isolating, you may cut access to backup infrastructure, identity providers, or management tools. For more surgical containment, prefer ID 4 (host isolation) where the scope is known.

Remove the compromised or suspected host from the network to stop lateral movement and cut the attacker's access while preserving the system for forensics. This is the most precise and lowest-impact containment action — it targets one system at a time without affecting the wider network.

The correct isolation method depends entirely on what is already deployed on the host. For compromised hosts, network-level isolation is always the right approach regardless of EDR status:

If EDR is already installed and active on the host:
Use the EDR console's network isolation command — Most enterprise EDR platforms provide a one-click network isolation that blocks all traffic except the EDR management channel. This is the fastest available method and leaves the agent running for live forensic collection.
The key distinction: you are not installing new software. You are sending a command to an agent that is already running. The attacker may already know the EDR is present, so triggering its isolation function does not introduce new detection risk. If the attacker responds to isolation by accelerating encryption, that is a reason to act faster — not a reason to delay isolation. A host left on the network after compromise is worse than a host that gets isolated even if the attacker reacts.

If EDR is NOT installed on the host:
Be careful deploying an EDR agent to a known or suspected compromised host during the incident. Deploying new software to a compromised host gives the attacker a visible signal of detection and may trigger immediate encryption or evidence destruction. Use network-level isolation instead:
• VLAN: reassign the switch port to an isolated VLAN with no routing to production
• Firewall ACL: apply a deny-all rule for the host's IP at the perimeter or core switch
• Cloud security group: remove all inbound and outbound rules for the instance, or apply a deny-all policy
• Physical disconnect: pull the network cable or disable the NIC via Device Manager
• Virtual machine: disconnect the vNIC in vCenter/ESXi without powering off the VM

WARNING: Regardless of isolation method — do NOT power off the host. Powering off destroys volatile memory (running processes, active network connections) that is critical for forensics. Isolate on the network and leave the system running.

Verify isolation worked: attempt a ping or connection to the host from another system — it should be unreachable. If using EDR-based isolation, confirm the console shows the host in an isolated state.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 3 (Isolate Network) covers segment-level isolation when multiple hosts in a zone are affected and host-by-host isolation is too slow. ID 6 (Deploy EDR) covers where to deploy EDR during an incident and why deployment to compromised hosts is specifically avoided.

actions.activity: AD on Prem + Entra — Disable Sync
actions.notes:
Disable the sync between on-premises Active Directory and Entra ID to prevent an on-prem compromise from propagating to the cloud, or a cloud compromise from writing back to on-premises infrastructure. While sync is active, an attacker with domain admin access can push changes directly into Entra ID, and a cloud-side compromise can write back credential changes to on-premises AD.

WARNING: TIMING CAVEAT: Disabling sync does not take effect instantly. Propagation is not immediate — do not assume sync is severed the moment you execute the command. Monitor Entra ID sync status to confirm the change has taken effect and treat the environment as still connected until confirmed.

Method 1: Entra ID Admin Portal (recommended during incident, no PowerShell dependency):
1. Sign in to Entra ID admin center (entra.microsoft.com) with a Global Administrator account
2. Navigate to: Identity > Hybrid Management > Microsoft Entra Connect
3. Under 'Microsoft Entra Connect Sync', select 'Manage'
4. Disable the sync scheduler or set the sync status to disabled
5. Confirm the sync status shows as disabled before proceeding

Method 2: PowerShell via Microsoft Graph SDK:
Connect-MgGraph -Scopes 'OnPremDirectorySynchronization.ReadWrite.All'
$syncId = (Get-MgDirectoryOnPremiseSynchronization).Id
Update-MgDirectoryOnPremiseSynchronization -OnPremisesDirectorySynchronizationId $syncId `
  -Configuration @{ customerRequestedSynchronizationInterval = 'PT0S' } `
  -Features @{ isSyncEnabled = $false }
Note: The Graph SDK module must be installed (Install-Module Microsoft.Graph) and the tenant may require a few minutes before the disabled state propagates to dependent services.

Method 3: Legacy PowerShell via the MSOnline module — use Set-MsolDirSyncEnabled. The MSOnline module is deprecated; Microsoft recommends the Entra admin portal or Graph SDK as preferred alternatives, but this command remains functional in most environments.

What this severs (sever all four before any credential rotation):
1. Active Directory to Entra ID user and group synchronisation
2. Password hash synchronisation: stops on-prem password changes writing to Entra ID
3. Pass-through authentication: on-prem AD no longer validates Entra ID sign-ins
4. Writeback flows: Entra ID password reset, group, and device writebacks to on-prem AD

NOTE: SEE ALSO: This action is a prerequisite for ID 13 (Rotate All Privileged Credentials). If sync is still active when credentials are rotated, the new credentials can be overwritten or read back by the attacker via the still-live sync channel.

Deploy endpoint detection and response technology to establish visibility across unaffected systems. Open source options are available for forensic triage and live response when a commercial EDR licence is not in place.

WARNING: DEPLOYMENT TARGET — RISK OF TIPPING OFF THE ATTACKER: Do NOT deploy EDR agents to known-compromised or suspected-compromised hosts during an active incident. Installing new software on a compromised host is a visible action — an attacker with administrative access can detect the installation event, terminate the process, or treat the deployment as a confirmed signal they have been detected. This may cause the attacker to immediately accelerate their timeline, triggering encryption or data destruction earlier than planned. This is the "going loud" risk: any detectable action on a host the attacker controls may cause them to move faster.

This warning applies specifically to deploying new agents. It does not apply to using an EDR agent that is already installed and running on a compromised host. If an agent is already present, it is already known to the attacker (or irrelevant to them), and using its network isolation feature to contain the host is the correct and fastest containment action — see ID 4 (Isolate Host).

For compromised hosts with no EDR agent, the right response is network-level isolation via VLAN reassignment, firewall ACL, cloud security group change, or physical cable pull. Do not delay isolation while attempting to deploy an agent. Isolation takes priority over instrumentation.

Deployment priority order for confirmed-clean systems:
1. Domain Controllers and authentication infrastructure — highest value for detecting attacker movement through the identity layer
2. File servers and backup infrastructure — early warning of encryption activity
3. Administrative workstations and jump servers — common attacker pivot points
4. General endpoint population — broad visibility sweep after critical assets are covered

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 4 (Isolate Host) covers the full isolation decision logic including the distinction between triggering an existing EDR agent versus deploying a new one.

Configure DNS sinkholing to redirect callback and exfiltration domains used by attacker tools to a controlled IP, preventing those tools from reaching their destinations even if they are already running on internal hosts. When the tool attempts to resolve its domain, it receives your sinkhole IP and the connection fails or is logged.

Priority domains to sinkhole:
• Remote access and RMM tools: AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect, Action1
• Exfiltration platforms: MegaSync, pCloud

Implementation options:
• DNS forwarder or resolver: create a zone override for the target domain pointing to a local sinkhole IP (e.g. 0.0.0.0 or a monitoring host)
• Firewall DNS filtering: block DNS queries to those domains at the perimeter
• Managed DNS security platforms: add domains to a custom block list

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 9 (Block Executables via AV/EDR) blocks the same tools at the endpoint execution layer. ID 55 (URL Filtering) blocks their download URLs. Use all three together for layered RMM and exfil tool coverage.

Enable PowerShell script block logging, module logging, and WMI activity logging on endpoints and servers to capture attacker commands and lateral movement activity from this point forward.

PowerShell logging via Group Policy:
Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows PowerShell
• Turn on Script Block Logging: Enabled
• Turn on Module Logging: Enabled (set module name to * to capture all modules)
Logged events appear under: Applications and Services Logs > Microsoft > Windows > PowerShell > Operational

WMI activity logging via Group Policy:
Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows Management Instrumentation
• Trace WMI activity: Enabled
Events appear under: Applications and Services Logs > Microsoft > Windows > WMI-Activity > Operational

WARNING: Enabling logging at this stage only captures activity from this point forward. All attacker actions taken before logging was enabled are permanently unlogged. Do not rely on PowerShell or WMI logs alone to reconstruct the full attack timeline — assume a visibility gap exists from initial access to the moment logging was activated. Supplement with EDR telemetry, Windows Event logs (4624, 4688, 7045), and network flow data to fill the gap.

actions.activity: Block Executables — Sync & RMM Tools (AV or EDR)
actions.notes:
Block known attacker sync, exfiltration, and RMM tools from executing using whatever application control mechanism is available. Filename blocking is fast but bypassable by renaming the binary, hash-based blocking is more reliable but requires the specific binary to generate the hash. Use both where possible.

Priority targets:
• Exfiltration tools: FileZilla, WinSCP, rclone, MegaSync, pCloud
• RMM/remote access tools: AnyDesk, Action1, ScreenConnect, Atera, Splashtop, TeamViewer, LogMeIn

Method 1: AV/EDR block rule (fastest, most available):
Add filenames or hashes to your EDR block list. Most EDR consoles allow you to add a SHA-256 hash or process name as a custom block rule. For filename-based blocking, use the exact executable name (e.g., rclone.exe, AnyDesk.exe).

Method 2: AppLocker deny rule:
GPO path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Application Control Policies > AppLocker > Executable Rules
Create a new Deny rule by: Publisher (certificate-based), Path (folder/filename), or Hash (most reliable). Publisher rules cover all versions signed by the same certificate. Hash rules cover only the specific version hashed.
After creating rules, set enforcement mode to Enforced for all rule collections.

Method 3: Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC):
See ID 10 for certificate-based blocking via WDAC.

WARNING: Filename blocking alone is insufficient, attackers routinely rename tools (e.g., rclone.exe renamed to svchost32.exe). Combine with hash blocking and DNS sinkholing (ID 7) for callback domains as a layered approach.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 7 (DNS Sinkhole) blocks the callback and exfil domains for these same tools at the network layer. ID 55 (URL Filtering) blocks the download URLs. Use all three together for maximum coverage.

Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) can deny execution of any application signed by a specific publisher certificate, blocking all current and future versions signed by that publisher without needing to update hash lists. This is more durable than hash-based blocking but requires more setup.

Steps:
1. Obtain a sample of the binary to block (e.g., AnyDesk.exe, rclone.exe)
2. Extract the publisher certificate information using Get-AuthenticodeSignature on the binary. Note the Subject and Issuer fields — these form the PublisherName in the deny rule.

3. Create a WDAC deny policy in XML with a Deny rule referencing the publisher name extracted in step 2. Microsoft's WDAC Wizard tool can generate the XML structure if you prefer a GUI approach.

4. Convert the XML policy to a binary and deploy it via MDM (Intune), Group Policy, or by placing the compiled policy file in the CodeIntegrity folder. Consult Microsoft's WDAC deployment documentation for the current deployment path and method for your OS version.

5. Restart the system or refresh the CI policy to apply the new rule.

WARNING: WDAC in block mode can break legitimate applications if the policy is too broad. Start with audit mode to validate impact before switching to enforce mode. Audit mode logs blocked executions to Event ID 3076 without actually blocking.

WARNING: WDAC policy deployment requires careful testing, a misconfigured policy can prevent the system from booting. Always test on a non-critical system first and maintain a recovery boot option.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 9 (Block Executables via AV or EDR) covers faster but less durable filename and hash-based blocking. ID 54 (ASR Rules) provides complementary execution controls via Defender.

Rotate, disable, or reduce the privileges of service accounts, accounts used by applications, scheduled tasks, services, and automated processes rather than by humans. Service accounts are a high-value lateral movement target because they often hold elevated privileges, are rarely monitored for interactive logon activity, and their credentials are frequently embedded in scripts or configuration files.

This action covers service accounts only. For privileged human accounts (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, local admins), see ID 13 (Accounts, Rotate All Privileged).

Priority targets:
• Service accounts with Domain Admin or local admin rights
• Accounts used by backup, monitoring, or endpoint management tools
• Accounts integrated with virtualisation platforms (vCenter service accounts)
• Accounts with permissions to create, modify, or link GPOs
• Accounts with access to file shares or databases across multiple systems

For each identified service account:
1. Determine if the account is actively required during the incident, if not, disable it
2. If required, rotate the credential to a long, randomly generated password
3. Remove any privileges that are not strictly necessary for the service to function
4. Where interactive and network logon are not required, enforce logon restrictions via Group Policy (see ID 43, Service Account Logon Restrictions)
5. Update all systems, scripts, and configuration files that reference the old credential

WARNING: SEQUENCING DEPENDENCY: Before rotating service account credentials, confirm the following action is complete:
• ID 60 (Isolate PAM / Password Vault): If the vault is compromised and you rotate service account credentials into it before isolating it, the attacker can re-read the new passwords. Vault isolation must happen first.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 13 (Rotate All Privileged) covers human privileged accounts. ID 43 (Service Account Logon Restrictions) enforces GPO-based logon restrictions to limit where service accounts can authenticate.

actions.activity: Limit Local Admin Rights (UAC Token Filtering)
actions.notes:
UAC token filtering restricts local accounts from using their full administrative privileges when authenticating over the network. When a local admin account connects remotely (via SMB, WMI, PsExec), Windows issues a filtered token that strips admin rights, preventing lateral movement even if the attacker holds valid local admin credentials. Domain accounts are not affected by this setting.

Registry key:
HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System
Value name: LocalAccountTokenFilterPolicy
Value type: REG_DWORD
Value: 0 (token filtering enabled, restricts remote admin via local accounts)

If the key does not exist, create it using regedit.exe or via the registry editor of your choice, then add the DWORD value with the name and data specified above.

WARNING: If the value is already set to 1 in your environment (a common misconfiguration), changing it to 0 will immediately restrict remote admin access via local accounts, which is the desired security outcome but may break existing remote management workflows that rely on local admin credentials over the network. Confirm impact before deploying at scale.

WARNING: This setting does not affect domain accounts or the built-in Administrator account (RID 500). For domain account restrictions, see ID 42 (Privileged Account Logon Restrictions).

Rotate credentials for all human privileged accounts, accounts held by people with elevated access to systems, infrastructure, and administrative consoles. This is one of the most critical containment actions in ransomware response. An attacker holding valid privileged credentials can undo every other containment measure, re-enter after isolation, modify GPOs, create new accounts, and access backup infrastructure. Credential rotation removes that persistence.

This action covers human privileged accounts only. For service accounts (application, scheduled task, and automated process accounts), see ID 11 (Service Accounts, Rotate / Disable).

Priority rotation order:
1. Domain Admin and Enterprise Admin accounts
2. Schema Admin accounts
3. All accounts with permissions to create, modify, or link GPOs
4. Local Administrator accounts on all domain-joined systems, use LAPS or equivalent to randomise and manage these at scale
5. Accounts with privileged access to virtualisation platforms (vCenter, ESXi)
6. Accounts with privileged access to backup infrastructure
7. All remaining accounts with local admin rights on any system

For each rotated account:
• Use a long, randomly generated password: minimum 25 characters
• Do not reuse any password from the pre-incident environment
• Do not store new passwords in the existing PAM vault or password manager until full containment is confirmed, use out-of-band secure storage
• Disable any account where rotation is not immediately possible rather than leaving the old credential active

WARNING: SEQUENCING DEPENDENCY: Before rotating credentials, confirm the following actions are complete:
• ID 60 (Isolate PAM / Password Vault): If the vault is compromised and you rotate credentials into it before isolating it, the attacker can re-read the new passwords. Vault isolation must happen first.
• ID 30 (Use Local Accounts): Do NOT store rotated passwords in the vault or password manager during the incident. Use local accounts and out-of-band secure storage for new credentials until full containment is confirmed.
• ID 5 (Disable AD-Entra Sync: if applicable): If AD-to-Entra sync is still running when on-premises credentials are rotated, the compromised on-prem AD can push dirty password hashes back into Entra ID and nullify the reset. Cut the sync first if both on-prem AD and Entra/SaaS are in scope.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 11 (Service Accounts) covers non-human accounts.
ID 29 (Privileged Account Password Rotations) is the parallel sweep for this action — it handles accounts not yet reached by this priority order and any account where rotation must be deferred. Run ID 13 and ID 29 in parallel: this action (ID 13) drives the priority sequence, ID 29 handles the remainder and the deferrals. ID 70 (Disable Compromised User Accounts) covers standard user accounts confirmed as compromised — run alongside this action.

Administrative shares (C$, ADMIN$, D$, IPC$) are used by ransomware operators for lateral movement: binding to remote systems, copying encryptors to target hosts, and executing payloads remotely via PsExec or direct SMB access. Disabling them removes that remote access and execution channel.

Fastest method — stop the Server service (immediate effect, no reboot required):
Open Services (services.msc) or run: net stop server
This disables all administrative and file sharing on the system immediately. It is the fastest containment option but also the broadest — it removes access to all shares including legitimate ones. Confirm operational dependencies before applying to servers.

For all other methods (registry, Group Policy via MSS Legacy template, restoration procedure) and the full Domain Controller impact warning, see ID 40 (Disable Administrative / Hidden Shares), which is the complete reference for this action.

Block outbound traffic on ports commonly used by attacker tools for C2 callbacks and data exfiltration. Egress filtering at the perimeter firewall or proxy stops attacker-controlled binaries from phoning home even if they are already running on internal hosts.

Priority ports to block outbound:
• C2 framework defaults: TCP 4444, 4445 (Metasploit), TCP 1337, 8443, 9001
• Cobalt Strike default: TCP 80, 443, 8080 (blends with web traffic, use proxy inspection or TLS inspection to catch)
• File transfer tools: TCP 21 (FTP), TCP 22 (SFTP/SCP if not needed), TCP 2049 (NFS)
• Legitimate-but-abused: restrict outbound TCP 443 from servers using allowlists where possible, after validating dependencies. Servers with no business reason to initiate HTTPS (file servers, DCs, backup agents) should have outbound 443 blocked by default with explicit exceptions for required destinations only — not a blanket block applied before dependencies are confirmed

WARNING: Before blocking TCP 443 from servers, build an explicit allow list for every legitimate outbound HTTPS dependency. Exceptions that must be carved out before applying the block:
• EDR and security tooling: EDR agents and other security tools communicate over TCP 443 to their cloud management consoles. Blocking this severs the EDR management channel and will disable remote isolation, forensic collection, and real-time detection on those hosts.
• Patch and update services: WSUS, SCCM/Intune endpoints, OS update URLs (windowsupdate.microsoft.com, etc.) — blocking these prevents security patches from applying during the incident.
• Backup agents: most backup platform agents use TCP 443 to reach cloud backup targets or management servers.
• Certificate validation and OCSP: servers performing TLS handshakes may need outbound TCP 443 to reach certificate authority OCSP responders.
• Any business-critical SaaS integration running on that server.

The correct approach is default-deny with explicit allow rules per destination IP or FQDN, not a blanket block. Apply the deny rule last, after the allow rules are confirmed and tested on one server before rolling out at scale.

Apply at the perimeter firewall for all internal subnets, and at a proxy or web gateway for user endpoints. For servers specifically, a default-deny-outbound policy with explicit allow rules for known destinations is the most effective posture.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 7 (DNS Sinkhole) blocks the callback domains. ID 59 (Block Outbound from Windows Binaries) applies application-layer outbound blocking on endpoints.

actions.activity: Apply Local Host Firewall Rules (Windows Firewall)
actions.notes:
Helps prevent lateral movement on endpoints. Apply the following Windows Firewall rules per host using the Windows Firewall with Advanced Security console or via netsh/PowerShell:

Port-based blocks (inbound and outbound):
• SMB: TCP/445, TCP/139, TCP/135
• RDP: TCP/3389

Application-based block (outbound):
• bitsadmin.exe: block via Windows Firewall outbound application rule, not a port rule. bitsadmin is a Windows command-line tool used to abuse the Background Intelligent Transfer Service for downloading attacker payloads. To block it: Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Outbound Rules > New Rule > Program > path to bitsadmin.exe > Block. Alternatively, see ID 59 (Block Outbound Connections from Specific Windows Binaries) which covers bitsadmin.exe alongside other high-risk binaries.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 38 (Restrict Lateral Movement from Endpoints) covers the same SMB/RDP ports via GPO-enforced rules, which scale across the domain automatically. If GPO deployment is available, prefer ID 38 for domain-wide coverage. Use this action (ID 16) for manual/per-host enforcement on specific systems where GPO cannot be applied quickly enough.

Image File Execution Options (IFEO) is a Windows registry mechanism that allows you to redirect the execution of a named binary to a different program, or to a non-existent path, which effectively prevents the target executable from launching. During an incident, this can be used to immediately block specific attacker tools on Domain Controllers or critical assets without requiring EDR, AppLocker, or WDAC to be already deployed.

This technique is particularly effective for blocking PsExec-based lateral movement on Domain Controllers, where psexesvc.exe is the server-side component that must execute for PsExec to work remotely.

Registry path:
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\\<executable name>

Create a new key named after the executable to block, then add a string value:
Value name: Debugger
Value type: REG_SZ
Value data: C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe
(or any non-existent path, the executable will fail to launch)

Registry editor (manual):
1. Open regedit.exe
2. Navigate to: HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options
3. Right-click > New > Key: name it after the executable (e.g., psexesvc.exe)
4. Inside the new key: Right-click > New > String Value, name it 'Debugger'
5. Set the value to any non-existent path (e.g., C:\block\block.exe)

Priority targets during ransomware IR:
• psexesvc.exe: blocks PsExec server-side component on DCs and servers
• wmic.exe: blocks WMI command-line tool used for lateral movement
• mshta.exe: blocks HTML application host used for script execution
• wscript.exe: blocks Windows Script Host (VBScript/JScript)
• cscript.exe: blocks command-line script host

To remove a block (restore normal execution):
Delete the registry key for the executable, or remove the Debugger value.

WARNING: Apply selectively to specific assets where the tool is not legitimately used. Blocking wmic.exe or wscript.exe domain-wide can break management scripts and monitoring agents that depend on these binaries. Test on one system before pushing via remote registry or GPO at scale.

NOTE: SEE ALSO, ID 54 (Attack Surface Reduction Rules) and ID 59 (Block Outbound Connections from Specific Windows Binaries) provide complementary application control coverage through Defender ASR and Windows Firewall outbound rules respectively.

Collect and preserve attacker tool artefacts and on-premises log sources before they are purged, overwritten, or lost. Ransomware operators routinely clear Windows Event Logs and application logs as part of anti-forensic activity.

Attacker tool logs:
Collect application logs from any identified attacker tools before the host is remediated. Priority sources:
• Sync and exfil tools: connection logs and file transfer history
• RMM tools: session logs and command history
• C2 framework artefacts: staged binaries and configuration files

Provide collected artefacts to your legal team — depending on jurisdiction you may be able to request takedowns or preserve data for law enforcement referral.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 52 (SaaS Compromise — Visibility and Log Ingestion) covers cloud and SaaS audit log collection and review, including M365 UAL, Entra ID sign-in logs, AWS CloudTrail, and GCP Audit Logs.

actions.activity: Unmount the LUN/Datastore from ESXi Cluster
actions.notes:
Unmount LUNs or datastores from the ESXi cluster to protect unencrypted VMDKs from being reached by ransomware processes running on the host. Once a datastore is unmounted at the ESXi layer, the VMs stored on it become inaccessible to any process on that host, including an active encryptor.

How to unmount a datastore:
Via vSphere Client: select the ESXi host > Configure > Storage > Datastores > select the datastore > right-click > Unmount. Repeat for all datastores on affected hosts.
Via vCenter: Storage view > right-click the datastore > Unmount Datastore > select the affected hosts.

WARNING: Do NOT reboot or shut down VMs before unmounting. Active ransomware processes on a running VM cannot modify files currently locked by the OS. That protection does not survive a reboot — some ransomware variants schedule encryption to run on next startup, delete Volume Shadow Copies, bypass VSS writers, or use a restart-based encryption sequence. A reboot hands the attacker the opportunity to complete what the lock prevented. Leave VMs running and unmount the datastore at the ESXi layer.

WARNING: Do not restore VMs from a compromised ESXi cluster until malicious processes have been identified and remediated on the host.

Limit which systems and accounts can reach ESXi hosts and vCenter. Unrestricted access to the management plane is the primary reason ransomware operators can encrypt VMDKs at scale, once they reach vCenter they can target all VMs at once.

Immediate steps:
1. Apply a host-based firewall rule on each ESXi host to allow management traffic only from specific admin IP ranges (jump servers, bastion hosts, PAWs). Use the ESXi host firewall settings in the vSphere Client or the ESXCLI network firewall commands — consult VMware documentation for current syntax.
2. At the network layer, restrict access to vCenter (port 443) and ESXi hosts (ports 443, 902) via firewall ACL to approved source IPs only
3. Disable any direct AD/LDAP authentication integration and switch to local accounts (see ID 25 and ID 30)

WARNING: Confirm which management systems legitimately need ESXi/vCenter access before applying IP restrictions, locking yourself out of vCenter during an active incident removes your ability to unmount datastores (ID 19) or enable lockdown mode (ID 22).

actions.activity: Ensure SSH Access to ESXi is Disabled
actions.notes:
Disable SSH on ESXi hosts to remove direct shell access. SSH on ESXi provides root-level command execution and is a primary attack path once an attacker has ESXi credentials. It is enabled for admin convenience and is frequently left on.

Via vSphere Client:
1. Select the ESXi host > Configure > System > Services
2. Select SSH > Stop, then set Startup Policy to 'Start and stop manually'
3. Repeat for ESXi Shell if enabled

WARNING: Disabling SSH does not terminate active SSH sessions already in progress. If an attacker is connected via SSH, disabling the service will prevent new connections but the existing session remains live until disconnected. Check for active SSH sessions before disabling by reviewing the ESXi host's network connections via the vSphere Client or the ESXCLI network connection list command. If an active session is found, terminate it before disabling the service.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 22 (ESXi Lockdown Mode) should be applied alongside SSH disable for layered access restriction.

Lockdown Mode restricts direct access to ESXi hosts, requiring all management operations to go through vCenter Server. This reduces the attack surface against direct host-level access by removing SSH, ESXi Shell, and — in Strict mode — DCUI as direct management paths.

Normal Lockdown Mode (what this action enables) disables SSH and ESXi Shell access for all accounts except those explicitly listed in the DCUI Access exception list. The DCUI itself remains available to accounts on that exception list, preserving a local emergency recovery path. Accounts NOT in the DCUI Access list, including root by default when lockdown is enabled, cannot use DCUI interactively. Strict Lockdown Mode additionally disables DCUI for all accounts without exception.

Enable via vSphere Client (Host UI):
1. Select the ESXi host in the vSphere Client inventory
2. Navigate to: Configure > System > Security Profile
3. Under Lockdown Mode, click Edit
4. Select Normal (restricts direct host access, vCenter management still works)
5. Click OK

Enable via ESXCLI: use the vim-cmd hostsvc/enable_lockdown command from a host shell or via SSH before SSH is disabled. Confirm the command in current VMware documentation as the syntax may vary by ESXi version.

Normal vs Strict Lockdown:
• Normal (this action): SSH and ESXi Shell are disabled. DCUI remains available to accounts on the DCUI Access exception list only — root is not on this list by default once lockdown is enabled. Recommended for incident response because it preserves a controlled local recovery path.
• Strict: DCUI is fully disabled for all accounts without exception. Use only if vCenter is confirmed accessible and an out-of-band recovery method exists. Do not use Strict mode if you may need emergency local console access.

WARNING: Enabling Lockdown Mode will disconnect any existing direct ESXi Shell or SSH sessions. Confirm vCenter connectivity before enabling, if vCenter is also compromised or unavailable, Lockdown Mode will leave the host unmanageable.

Reference: https://kb.vmware.com/s/article/1008077

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 21 (Disable SSH on ESXi) should be applied alongside Lockdown Mode for layered access restriction.

Enable the execInstalledOnly advanced configuration option on ESXi hosts to restrict execution to binaries installed as part of the ESXi image. This prevents attacker-dropped tools and encryptors from running directly on the ESXi shell, even if the attacker has obtained root access.

WARNING: POST-FORENSICS STEP ONLY — SEQUENCING DEPENDENCY. Do not apply this action until after all forensic memory captures and disk snapshots are complete AND ID 19 (Unmount LUN/Datastore) has been executed. Two separate risks must be managed:
1. The required host reboot will destroy volatile memory evidence — running processes, active network connections, and encryption keys held in RAM. All forensic collection must be complete before rebooting.
2. execInstalledOnly only blocks new tool drops — a ransomware process already running retains full write access to any mounted VMDK and can continue encrypting. Unmounting the datastore (ID 19) is what actually stops active encryption. This setting provides defence-in-depth after the datastores are protected and forensic collection is complete.
If either condition is not yet met, rely on ID 19 alone to protect VMDKs without triggering a reboot.

Enable via vSphere Client:
Select the ESXi host > Configure > System > Advanced System Settings > search for VMkernel.Boot.execInstalledOnly > set to True.

Only reboot to activate the setting after all of the following are confirmed:
1. All forensic memory captures from the ESXi host are complete
2. All disk snapshots or VMDK images required for evidence are taken
3. ID 19 (Unmount LUN/Datastore) has been executed to protect active VMs
4. The decision to reboot has been confirmed with the IR lead

Reference: VMware vSphere Security Configuration Guide, VMkernel.Boot.execInstalledOnly.

Require multi-factor authentication for all access to ESXi hosts and vCenter. Default ESXi and vCenter deployments use single-factor authentication, a compromised password is sufficient for full administrative access.

ESXi does not natively support MFA. Enforcement requires one of:

Option 1: vCenter with external identity provider (recommended):
Configure vCenter to authenticate via an external IdP (Okta, Entra ID, ADFS) using SAML. The IdP enforces MFA before issuing a token to vCenter. Path: vCenter > Administration > Single Sign On > Configuration > Identity Provider

Option 2: Smart card / PIV authentication:
vCenter supports smart card authentication via the vSphere Client. Requires PKI infrastructure and is most common in government/regulated environments.

Option 3: Jump server with MFA as the access gateway:
If native MFA on vCenter is not deployable during the incident, restrict all vCenter access to a single hardened jump server that itself requires MFA. Combined with IP restriction (ID 20), this enforces MFA at the perimeter.

WARNING: During an active incident, native IdP integration is unlikely to be deployable in time. Option 3 (MFA-gated jump server) is the fastest available control. Prioritise ID 20 (restrict ESXi access by IP) and ID 25 (sever AD integration) alongside this action.

Remove Active Directory and LDAP authentication integrations from vCenter and ESXi hosts, then switch to local accounts for all virtualisation management access. When AD integration is active, an attacker with domain admin credentials can authenticate directly to vCenter — removing the integration severs that path.

Steps:
1. In vCenter, navigate to Administration > Single Sign On > Configuration > Identity Sources. Remove the Active Directory or LDAP identity source.
2. Confirm at least one local vCenter administrator account exists and the password is known before removing the AD integration — you must have a working local account to retain management access.
3. Switch all ongoing vCenter operations to the local account.
4. Apply the same principle to all other AD-integrated infrastructure platforms: backup systems, network management consoles, and PAM solutions (see ID 30).

WARNING: SEQUENCING DEPENDENCY: Complete ID 30 (Use Local Accounts for Administrative Access) before this action. If AD integration is severed before local accounts are confirmed and accessible, you risk losing all administrative access to vCenter during the incident.

Ensuring the virtualisation platform forwards logs to your SIEM or centralised logging platform is a prerequisite for detecting attacker activity in the ESXi/vCenter layer. Without it, authentication attempts, configuration changes, and suspicious VM operations are invisible.

What to capture:
• Authentication events (successful and failed logins to vCenter and ESXi hosts)
• Authorisation events (privilege escalation, role assignment changes)
• Configuration changes (VM creation/deletion, network changes, snapshot operations)
• Administrative actions (user account creation, permission modifications)

Configure syslog forwarding from ESXi to your SIEM:
Via vSphere Client:
1. Select the ESXi host > Configure > System > Advanced System Settings
2. Search for Syslog.global.logHost
3. Set value to: udp://<SIEM-IP>:514 or tcp://<SIEM-IP>:1514
4. Apply and restart the syslog service using the ESXCLI syslog reload command or via the vSphere Client service restart option.

Configure vCenter audit logging:
vCenter Server > Administration > Syslog Configuration
Add your SIEM as a syslog target. vCenter audit events include all API calls, UI actions, and configuration changes.

WARNING: Enabling logging at this stage only captures activity from this point forward. All attacker actions before logging was configured are unrecorded. Do not rely solely on vCenter/ESXi logs to reconstruct the full attack timeline, supplement with network flow data and any EDR telemetry available.

Shorten the vpxuser password expiry interval in vCenter to reduce the window of exposure if the credential is captured. The vpxuser account is the automated service account vCenter uses to authenticate to each ESXi host. Its default password expiry is 30 days — shortening this forces more frequent automatic rotation.

Shorten the expiry in vCenter:
The navigation path for the password policy setting varies by vSphere version:
• vSphere 6.x / 7.x: vCenter > Administration > Single Sign On > Configuration > Local Accounts > Password policy
• vSphere 8.x: vCenter > Administration > Single Sign On > Configuration > Password Policy (the Local Accounts submenu was reorganised in vSphere 8.0)
In both versions, locate the maximum password lifetime setting and reduce it from the default 30 days to a shorter interval (e.g. 24 hours). vCenter automatically rotates the vpxuser password on each ESXi host according to this schedule. Confirm the current path in VMware documentation for your deployed version.

On ESXi 8.0 and later, the vpxuser account can also be restricted from interactive shell access via the host's advanced security settings.

WARNING: Do NOT manually reset the vpxuser password. Manual resets break the trust relationship between vCenter and the ESXi host and cause vCenter to lose the ability to manage that host. Password rotation must be performed exclusively through vCenter's automatic rotation mechanism.

Identify all privileged accounts in the environment and reduce both their number and scope of access. Attackers depend on over-privileged accounts to move laterally and escalate — reducing the number of accounts with Domain Admin, local admin, and GPO modification rights limits the damage any single compromised credential can cause.

Accounts to identify and review:
• Accounts assigned default privileged AD roles (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins)
• Accounts with permissions to create, modify, or link GPOs
• Accounts with explicit permissions on Domain Controllers
• Accounts assigned privileged roles on virtualisation platforms
• Accounts with local administrative access across a large scope of endpoints

Reduction steps:
1. Remove any account from privileged groups where the access is not operationally required
2. Restrict service accounts to the minimum permissions required for their function
3. Enforce logon restrictions via Group Policy for service accounts that do not require interactive or remote logon:
Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment
• Deny log on locally (SeDenyInteractiveLogonRight)
• Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services (SeDenyRemoteInteractiveLogonRight)
4. For service accounts used on a single system, restrict them to that system via Active Directory Users and Computers > Account tab > Log On To

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 42 (Privileged Account Logon Restrictions) and ID 43 (Service Account Logon Restrictions) enforce GPO-based logon restrictions for accounts identified here.

Rotate credentials for all privileged accounts. Execute this action in parallel with ID 13 (Accounts — Rotate All Privileged Priority Order), which drives the priority sequence from Domain Admins downward. This action covers the accounts not yet reached by ID 13's priority order and any account where immediate rotation is not possible. Together they cover the full privileged account population — run both concurrently with separate responders where possible, not as alternatives.

Scope for this action:
• Accounts in ID 13's priority list where rotation is blocked or delayed (system dependencies, change window constraints, missing credentials) — disable these accounts temporarily while waiting to rotate them
• Accounts not covered in ID 13's 7-level order but that carry elevated permissions in specific systems or platforms
• Any account where rotation cannot be completed immediately — disable it rather than leaving the old credential active

Apply the same rotation rules as ID 13: minimum 25 characters, no reuse, out-of-band storage only.

The same sequencing dependencies apply as ID 13 — complete ID 60 (Isolate PAM), ID 30 (Use Local Accounts), and ID 5 (Disable AD-Entra Sync) before rotating any credentials. See ID 13 for the full dependency explanation.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 13 (Accounts — Rotate All Privileged Priority Order) drives the main rotation sequence — this action fills in what ID 13 defers or cannot reach immediately. ID 11 (Service Accounts) covers non-human accounts.

actions.activity: Use Local Accounts for Administrative Access
actions.notes:
Switch all infrastructure management access to local administrator accounts and sever Active Directory integrations from critical platforms. When AD is compromised, any platform using AD for authentication inherits that compromise. Removing the integration forces the attacker to find alternative credentials for each platform independently.

Integrations to sever immediately:
1. VMware vCenter: Administration > Single Sign On > Configuration > Identity Sources — remove the AD identity source and switch to the local vCenter administrator account
2. PAM / Password vault: disable AD integration in the PAM console and switch to local PAM service accounts
3. Backup solution: remove AD-authenticated admin accounts from the backup console and use local backup admin credentials
4. Cloud IAM: review and remove AD-federated roles from AWS, Azure, or GCP where the federation trust can be abused via the compromised AD

Confirm a working local administrator account exists on each platform before severing the AD integration — access to the platform must not be lost during the response.

Local accounts must use long randomly generated passwords not stored in the compromised vault, have MFA enforced where supported, and be limited to the minimum two accounts needed for operational resilience.

actions.activity: Identify and Harden Single-Factor Authentication Access Methods
actions.notes:
Audit all externally accessible platforms for single-factor authentication and enforce MFA across all of them. Where MFA cannot be enforced immediately, restrict external access to a small set of trusted IP ranges as a temporary control.

Platforms to audit and harden:
• VPN and remote access gateways
• Virtual Desktop Infrastructure (VDI)
• Cloud management consoles (AWS, Azure, GCP)
• SaaS administrative consoles
• Identity providers and SSO portals

For incidents involving vishing, MFA manipulation, or social engineering, standard push notification and SMS MFA are insufficient — these are bypassed via MFA fatigue attacks or SIM swapping. Escalate to phishing-resistant MFA for all privileged and administrative accounts:
• Remove SMS, phone call, push notification, and email as accepted authentication factors for admin accounts
• Require FIDO2 security keys or passkeys for all accounts with privileged roles
• For standard users: require authenticator apps with FIDO2 or passkey support
• Enforce multi-context criteria in Conditional Access: validate identity AND device compliance AND known egress location as part of every authentication decision
• Okta: require phishing-resistant authenticator policy for admin console access
• Entra ID: Conditional Access policy requiring phishing-resistant MFA strength for privileged roles
• Google Workspace: enforce Advanced Protection for high-risk users and require security keys for 2SV

Use when a compromise is suspected but social engineering of the help desk or active SSPR abuse has not yet been confirmed. This is the measured version, validate and harden the process without fully locking it down.

Actions:
• Temporarily disable or add friction to external self-service password reset methods
• Require positive identity verification before any account change
• Implement out-of-band verification (callback to registered number) for high-risk changes
• Exclude privileged accounts from self-service password reset tools
• Monitor for Event ID 4723 (user-initiated reset) and 4724 (admin-initiated reset) in Active Directory
• For Entra ID: monitor 'Reset password (self-service)' and 'Reset password (by admin)' audit events

WARNING: If social engineering or active SSPR abuse is confirmed or suspected, escalate immediately to ID 61 (Lock External SSPR and Harden Help Desk Verification), that is the emergency version of this action.

actions.activity: Review / Restrict MFA Device Self-Registration Processes
actions.notes:
Review and validate security controls for MFA device registrations and modifications. Prevents attackers from registering their own MFA devices to maintain persistence.

ALERT: ACTIVE INCIDENT: PAUSE ENROLLMENT IMMEDIATELY:
During an active incident involving vishing or MFA manipulation, do not just review, temporarily DISABLE the ability for users to self-register or enroll new devices entirely:
• Okta: Disable self-service MFA enrollment in Security > Authenticators > Enrollment policy, set to admin-only enrollment
• Entra ID: Restrict MFA registration under Authentication Methods > Registration campaign, require admin initiation only
• Google Workspace: Disable end-user self-enrollment of 2SV methods in Admin Console > Security > 2-Step Verification
• Apply this to ALL users immediately: communicate to end users that MFA changes must be requested via a verified helpdesk channel
• Log every MFA registration event during the incident window and treat any new enrollment as a high-priority alert requiring investigation
• Do NOT re-enable self-service enrollment until the identity provider is confirmed clean and all attacker-registered devices have been removed

Scanning your external attack surface during an active incident can quickly surface the likely initial access vector, an exposed RDP port, an unpatched VPN appliance, an internet-facing Citrix instance, or a misconfigured management console. This information directly informs which containment actions to prioritise.

Quick scan using an external attack surface management tool or a free internet scanning service (no account required for basic searches):
1. Search for your organisation's public IP ranges or primary domain using your organisation name or CIDR ranges as search terms.

2. Filter results for high-risk services:
 • RDP: port 3389, if exposed, cross-reference with ID 39
 • SMB: port 445, direct internet exposure is a mass-propagation risk
 • VPN appliances: port 443, 4443, 10443, check for known CVEs on the detected product version
 • Citrix/VDI: port 443 with Citrix headers, cross-reference with ID 57
 • ESXi/vCenter: port 443, 902, 9443, cross-reference with ID 20 and ID 21
 • Remote management consoles (iDRAC, iLO, IPMI): ports 623, 443, 80

3. For any exposed service on an unpatched appliance, check the vendor's CVE list for the detected version, recently disclosed critical CVEs on internet-facing appliances are the most common initial access vector across all major ransomware groups.

WARNING: This is a visibility action, not a containment action. The information it produces should immediately feed into decisions about which isolation, patching, or restriction actions to prioritise, it has no direct containment value on its own.

Disconnect network links between the compromised environment and other sites, data centres, and cloud environments to prevent ransomware from spreading across boundaries. Ransomware operators routinely pivot from an on-premises breach into connected cloud environments and remote offices via these trusted links.

Common integration types to review and sever:
• IPsec/VPN tunnels to remote offices and data centres, disable on the firewall or VPN concentrator
• AWS Direct Connect / Azure ExpressRoute / GCP Cloud Interconnect, disable the virtual interface or circuit at the cloud portal
• SD-WAN fabric: isolate the affected site via the SD-WAN management console
• BGP peering: withdraw routes to/from the affected segment at the core router
• Cloud VPC peering: remove the peering association in the cloud console

WARNING: Severing site-to-site links will cut off remote users and connected offices. Confirm with business leadership before executing. Prioritise cutting links toward unaffected environments, it is better to isolate the compromised site than to allow the spread to clean infrastructure.

actions.activity: Restrict or Shutdown Virtual Private Network (VPN)
actions.notes:
Immediately restrict VPN access to a named list of essential users only, do not leave VPN open to the broad user population during an active incident.

Step 1: Build the critical users list:
Convene a rapid decision with IT leadership, IR lead, and business leadership. The default list is: IR team members, SOC analysts, key IT infrastructure staff (network, identity, backup), and any executives or legal/finance roles with operational necessity during the incident. Document every name and business justification, this list is your audit trail.

Step 2: Apply the restriction technically:
• Create or update an AD/Entra group (e.g., VPN-IR-Approved) containing only the listed users
• Update the VPN policy (firewall rule, NAC policy, or IdP Conditional Access) to allow VPN authentication only for members of that group
• Block or disable VPN profiles/connections for all other users immediately
• If the VPN uses RADIUS or LDAP for authentication, update the group filter at the authentication server

Step 3: Terminate existing active sessions:
Do not only block new connections, actively terminate all currently connected VPN sessions for users not on the approved list. Attackers with valid credentials may already be connected. Check your VPN console for active session management.

Step 4: Enforce phishing-resistant MFA for all retained VPN users:
All users on the approved list must authenticate using MFA before VPN access is granted. For incidents involving vishing or social engineering:
• Remove SMS and push notification as accepted MFA factors for VPN, these are bypassable via social engineering
• Require FIDO2 / hardware token or certificate-based authentication where possible
• If phishing-resistant MFA is not available, require a separate out-of-band verification step before granting access

Step 5: Block non-compliant and unmanaged devices:
Restrict VPN access to managed, compliant devices only. An attacker with stolen credentials connecting from their own device bypasses this control entirely if device compliance is not enforced. Use Conditional Access policies, device posture checks from your NAC or MDM solution, or certificate-based authentication to enforce managed-device-only access.

Step 6: Review the list regularly during the incident:
Reassess the approved user list at every shift change. Remove users who no longer require remote access. Tighten to shutdown if the situation escalates to active encryption.

WARNING: If Block ALL Traffic (ID 37) is executed, this action is superseded, VPN traffic is included in the all-traffic block. Do not maintain this list in parallel with ID 37.

WARNING: HIGH IMPACT: This action accepts the full business disruption of cutting all connectivity in exchange for stopping active exfiltration or encryption. All user access will be severed. Confirm with business leadership before executing. An alternative with lower impact is to block all OUTBOUND traffic only, which stops exfiltration and C2 callbacks while preserving inbound access for recovery teams.

Only appropriate when:
• Exfiltration is actively ongoing and cannot be stopped by targeted blocks
• Encryption is confirmed in progress and host isolation alone is insufficient to contain it
• The cost of continued attacker access outweighs the cost of full connectivity loss

NOTE: REDUNDANCY NOTE: If this action is executed, the following actions become redundant and do not need to be separately completed:
• ID 3 (Isolate Network): blocking all traffic subsumes network segment isolation
• ID 36 (Restrict/Shutdown VPN): VPN traffic is included in the all-traffic block
Do not waste time executing those separately once this action is in place.

NOTE: DECISION GATE: ID 51 (Firewall QoS Delay) is the mutually exclusive alternative strategy: choosing to delay and monitor the attacker's C2 channel is incompatible with blocking all traffic. If ID 51 is currently active, executing this action will alert the attacker that they have been detected. Make a conscious decision to abandon the delay tactic before executing this action.

Block workstation-to-workstation and workstation-to-non-DC/non-File-Server traffic:
• SMB (TCP/445, TCP/135, TCP/139)
• Remote Desktop Protocol (TCP/3389)
• Windows Remote Management / Remote PowerShell (TCP/80, TCP/5985, TCP/5986)
• WMI (dynamic DCOM port range)

Quick enforcement via netsh (per host or scripted at scale before GPO can be deployed):
netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group="File and Printer Sharing" new enable=no
netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group="Remote Desktop" new enable=no
netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group="Windows Remote Management" new enable=no
netsh advfirewall firewall set rule group="windows management instrumentation (wmi)" new enable=no

Or disable PowerShell Remoting directly:
Disable-PSRemoting -Force

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 16 (Apply Local Host Firewall Rules) covers the same SMB/RDP ports via manual per-host Windows Firewall configuration. This action (ID 38) applies the equivalent rules via GPO for domain-wide coverage. Do not execute both, choose based on speed and scope: GPO (this action) for domain-wide enforcement, ID 16 for targeted manual application on specific hosts. ID 41 (WinRM Hardening) disables WinRM at the service layer — run alongside this action for defence-in-depth.

Harden Remote Desktop Protocol access to prevent it from being used for initial access or lateral movement. RDP on internet-facing systems is a primary entry vector across most ransomware groups.

1. Enforce MFA for RDP: require multi-factor authentication before a session is established, via Remote Desktop Gateway with Entra ID / RADIUS integration, or via a third-party MFA wrapper.

2. Enable Network Level Authentication (NLA): requires authentication before a full RDP session is established, protecting against brute force and unauthenticated exposure. GPO path:
Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Remote Desktop Services > Remote Desktop Session Host > Security >
Require user authentication for remote connections by using NLA: Enabled

WARNING: NLA uses CredSSP to pass authentication requests from the initiating system. CredSSP stores credentials in LSA memory on the source host and those credentials may remain in memory even after the user logs off. On a compromised initiating host, enabling NLA can therefore expose newly entered credentials through LSA memory. Apply NLA on the RDP server side to enforce pre-authentication, but do not use a compromised or suspected host as an RDP client while CredSSP is active.

3. Restrict administrative accounts from RDP on internet-facing systems. GPO path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment > Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services, add Domain Admins and other privileged groups.

NOTE: Source: Mandiant Ransomware Protection and Containment Strategies.

Ransomware operators use administrative and hidden shares (ADMIN$, C$, D$, IPC$) to bind to endpoints throughout the environment and deploy encryptors — staging payloads to target systems and executing them remotely, often via PsExec or direct SMB access. Disabling these shares removes that lateral deployment channel. Note that this action targets the attacker's movement and execution path. File shares — where ransomware actually encrypts data — are a separate concern and are not addressed by disabling administrative shares.

WARNING: IMPACT WARNING: Disabling administrative and hidden shares on Domain Controllers will significantly impact domain functionality. Group Policy, SYSVOL replication, remote management tools, and many monitoring agents depend on these shares. Apply to workstations and member servers first. Only apply to DCs if the situation is critical and the operational impact is accepted.

If PsExec is used in the environment, disabling ADMIN$ prevents it from being used to remotely deploy or execute anything on endpoints — PsExec requires ADMIN$ to copy and launch its service component on the remote host.

Method 1: Registry (workstations):
HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters
DWORD Name: AutoShareWks, Value: 0

Method 2: Registry (servers):
HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters
DWORD Name: AutoShareServer, Value: 0

A restart of the Server service or a system reboot is required for the registry change to take effect. If an immediate effect is needed without a reboot, use Method 3.

Method 3: Stop the Server service (immediate effect, no reboot required).

WARNING: Stopping the Server service disables ALL file and print sharing on the system immediately, including any legitimate shares. This is the fastest option but has the broadest impact. Confirm operational dependencies before applying to servers.

Method 4: Group Policy (domain-wide, MSS Legacy template):
Requires the Microsoft Security Compliance Toolkit MSS Legacy GPO template to be imported into Group Policy Management.

For workstations:
Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > MSS (Legacy) > MSS: (AutoShareWks) Enable Administrative Shares (not recommended) > Set to: Disabled

For servers:
Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > MSS (Legacy) > MSS: (AutoShareServer) Enable Administrative Shares (not recommended) > Set to: Disabled

To restore shares after the incident: delete the AutoShareWks or AutoShareServer registry value (do not set to 1 — deleting the value restores default behaviour), then restart the Server service.

NOTE: Source: Mandiant Ransomware Protection and Containment Strategies.
NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 14 (Disable File Shares) covers the same capability with a focus on the fastest immediate method (stopping the Server service). This action (ID 40) is the more complete reference.

Disable Windows Remote Management and PowerShell Remoting to prevent attackers from using these channels for lateral movement and remote command execution. WinRM is the transport for PowerShell Remoting and is frequently abused in ransomware deployments alongside SMB.

GPO path (domain-wide):
Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Windows Remote Management (WinRM) > WinRM Service >
Allow remote server management through WinRM: Disabled

WARNING: Disabling WinRM will break remote PowerShell sessions, Desired State Configuration (DSC), and monitoring agents that use WS-Management. Confirm which management tools depend on WinRM before applying at scale to avoid breaking your own visibility and management capability.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 38 (Restrict Lateral Movement from Endpoints) blocks WinRM ports TCP/5985 and TCP/5986 at the firewall level as a complementary control.

Block privileged accounts from authenticating to standard workstations, laptops, and common access servers (e.g. VDI). During ransomware incidents, attackers holding privileged credentials use them to log into workstations and spread laterally, restricting where those accounts can authenticate contains the blast radius even if the credentials themselves have not yet been rotated.

This is enforced via Group Policy User Rights Assignment. Apply to a GPO linked at the domain or OU level targeting workstations and common access servers. Do NOT apply these restrictions to Domain Controllers or administrative jump servers, privileged accounts must retain the ability to log into those systems to manage the incident.

GPO path:
Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment

Settings to configure, add privileged account groups to each:

Deny log on locally
Prevents privileged accounts from logging in interactively at the console of workstations and common servers.
Add: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, and any other privileged groups

Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services
Prevents privileged accounts from connecting via RDP to workstations and common servers.
Add: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, and any other privileged groups

Deny access to this computer from the network
Prevents privileged accounts from connecting via SMB, WMI, PsExec, or any network-based authentication to workstations and common servers.
Add: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, Schema Admins, and any other privileged groups

Deny log on as a batch job
Prevents privileged accounts from being used for scheduled tasks on workstations.
Add: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and any other privileged groups

Deny log on as a service
Prevents privileged accounts from being registered as service accounts on workstations.
Add: Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and any other privileged groups

WARNING: Before applying, verify that no legitimate administrative processes on workstations depend on Domain Admin or other privileged accounts logging in locally or via network. In most well-managed environments this dependency should not exist, but confirm before applying at scale to avoid breaking management tooling during the incident.

WARNING: These restrictions apply to the systems the GPO targets, not to the accounts themselves. Privileged accounts remain usable on Domain Controllers and designated admin workstations (PAWs) where the GPO is not applied.

NOTE: Source: Mandiant Ransomware Protection and Containment Strategies; Google Threat Intelligence UNC3944 Hardening Guidance.
SEE ALSO, ID 43 (Service Account Logon Restrictions) applies the same GPO mechanism to service accounts. ID 28 (Privileged Account Identification and Reduction) covers identifying which accounts require these restrictions.

Service accounts used by applications, monitoring tools, backup agents, and scheduled tasks rarely need interactive or remote logon rights, but they frequently have them by default. Attackers who compromise a service account credential will attempt to use it for RDP, network-based authentication, or direct console logon to move laterally. Enforcing logon restrictions via GPO removes those authentication paths without disrupting the legitimate function of the service.

Apply to endpoints and servers where the service account operates but does not require interactive or network-based human logon. Do not apply blanket restrictions without first confirming the account is not used for remote administration on that system.

GPO path:
Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Local Policies > User Rights Assignment

Settings to configure, add the service account or its containing security group:

Deny log on locally
Prevents the service account from being used for interactive console logon.
Add: the specific service account or group

Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services
Prevents the service account from being used for RDP connections.
Add: the specific service account or group

Deny access to this computer from the network
Prevents the service account from authenticating via SMB, WMI, PsExec, or other network-based protocols on endpoints where it is not needed.
Add: the specific service account or group

WARNING: Apply per-account and per-scope. A service account that runs a backup agent on server A may legitimately need network access to server A but not to workstations. Scope the GPO accordingly, do not apply a blanket domain-wide restriction without mapping which systems each service account legitimately needs to reach.

WARNING: Do NOT add service accounts to the Protected Users security group as a substitute for these restrictions, Protected Users disables NTLM and Kerberos delegation, which will break most services that rely on them. Use User Rights Assignment instead (see ID 44, Protected Users Security Group for the relevant warning).

NOTE: Source: Mandiant Ransomware Protection and Containment Strategies.
SEE ALSO, ID 42 (Privileged Account Logon Restrictions) applies the same mechanism to human privileged accounts. ID 28 (Privileged Account Identification and Reduction) covers identifying which service accounts carry excessive privilege.

Add privileged user accounts to the Protected Users security group to enforce automatic credential protections. Members of the group cannot authenticate using NTLM, cannot use Kerberos delegation, and cannot use RC4 encryption — protections that prevent credential theft and pass-the-hash attacks against those accounts.

Requirement: domain functional level Windows Server 2012 R2 or higher.

To add an account: Active Directory Users and Computers > find the account > Properties > Member Of > Add > Protected Users.

WARNING: Do NOT add service accounts to this group. Service accounts commonly rely on NTLM, Kerberos delegation, or RC4 — all of which are disabled for Protected Users members. Adding a service account will immediately break any service that depends on these mechanisms.

WARNING: SERVICE DISRUPTION RISK: Adding any account to Protected Users disables NTLM, Kerberos unconstrained and constrained delegation, and RC4 encryption for that account. Any application or system that depends on these mechanisms for that account will break immediately. Test in staging if possible. If staging is not available during an active incident, accept the disruption risk as a conscious decision, document it, notify the business, and retain the rollback: removing the account from the group restores previous behaviour.

WARNING: RDP SILENT FAILURE — Protected Users members can only authenticate via Kerberos, which requires a direct network path to a Domain Controller. If the user attempts RDP from a network segment that cannot reach a DC at authentication time, the RDP session will silently fail with a generic authentication error rather than a clear message about NTLM being disabled. This is a common silent failure mode that is difficult to diagnose under incident pressure.

The mitigation is not to avoid adding accounts to Protected Users — it is to ensure at least one Domain Controller remains reachable from every segment where those accounts need to log in. ID 2 (Isolate One Domain Controller) is designed to preserve this: it isolates one DC as a clean reference point while keeping other DCs operational for authentication. Before adding admin accounts to Protected Users, confirm that the domain has at least one non-isolated, reachable DC, and that all segments those accounts need to reach via RDP have a routed path to it.
