Disable WDigest authentication to prevent Windows from caching cleartext credentials in LSASS memory. When WDigest is enabled, Windows stores a reversible copy of user passwords in memory — an attacker with access to LSASS (via credential dumping tools) can extract those plaintext passwords directly. Disabling WDigest removes that exposure.

Enable via Group Policy using the Microsoft Security Guide template:
Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > MS Security Guide > WDigest Authentication > set to Disabled.

The Microsoft Security Guide GPO template is part of the Microsoft Security Compliance Toolkit. Import the ADMX template into Group Policy Management before this setting becomes available in the console.

NOTE: WDigest is disabled by default on Windows 8.1 and Windows Server 2012 R2 and later. Apply this action for environments running older Windows versions or where the setting may have been explicitly re-enabled.

WARNING: ATTACKER RE-ENABLEMENT RISK: An attacker who retains registry write access to a host can re-enable WDigest at any time during the incident by setting the registry value HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\WDigest\UseLogonCredential to 1. Once re-enabled, the next interactive logon to that host will cache the user's cleartext password in LSASS memory. This is an active technique used during incidents to capture credentials entered by responders after initial access was achieved. Disabling WDigest via GPO is not sufficient if the attacker can still write to that registry key directly on a compromised host. Apply ID 45 in conjunction with host isolation (ID 4) to prevent the attacker from reaching the host to re-enable it. After applying the GPO, monitor for Event ID 4657 (registry value modified) on critical hosts — a change to the UseLogonCredential value is a high-confidence indicator of active attacker presence on that host.

Review all Group Policy Objects for unauthorised modifications. Attackers with domain admin access commonly modify or create GPOs to deploy ransomware payloads, disable security tools, or create persistence via startup scripts.

List recently modified GPOs using Get-GPO in PowerShell or the Group Policy Management Console (GPMC) — sort by modification time and compare against your change management records. Focus on GPOs modified in the last 30 to 90 days and cross-reference against your change management records. Investigate any GPO with:
• Startup or logon scripts pointing to unusual paths or UNC shares
• Software installation policies deploying unknown packages
• Security settings disabling Windows Defender, audit logging, or firewall
• Scheduled tasks created via GPO targeting domain-joined systems

Review which accounts have GPO creation/modification rights using Get-GPPermission in PowerShell or by checking Delegation settings in the GPMC for each GPO.

NOTE: Source: Mandiant Ransomware Protection and Containment Strategies. SEE ALSO, ID 47 (Monitor GPO Changes via Event ID 5136) for ongoing detection.

Monitor Domain Controller security event logs for Event ID 5136, which records every modification to Active Directory objects including Group Policy Objects. This is the primary detection mechanism for GPO-based persistence and ransomware deployment activity.

Prerequisite, enable the audit policy on Domain Controllers:
GPO path: Computer Configuration > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Advanced Audit Policy Configuration > DS Access >
Audit Directory Service Changes: Success and Failure

Or via auditpol on each DC — consult Microsoft documentation for the current syntax to enable the Directory Service Changes subcategory under DS Access.

Query recent 5136 events using Get-WinEvent with a FilterHashtable targeting the Security log and Event ID 5136, scoped to the DC and a time window covering the incident period.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 46 (Review Group Policy Objects) for manual review of GPO modification timestamps. Both actions should be run together.

Map all systems and dependencies that backup recovery relies on before attempting any restoration. Recovery efforts that proceed without this mapping frequently stall or fail because a required component — an authentication server, an encryption key store, a DNS resolver — is itself unavailable or compromised.

Dependencies to confirm are available and uncompromised before recovery begins:
• Identity Provider (Active Directory or cloud IdP) for authenticating to recovery consoles and restored systems
• MFA platform — required to access backup software or management consoles
• Administrative platforms: bastion hosts, SAWs, PAM systems
• Emergency access credentials, stored out-of-band
• DNS — required for name resolution during system restoration
• Storage connectivity: confirm on-premises and cloud storage paths are accessible
• Offline backup media: confirm physical location and accessibility
• Encryption keys for backup data, stored separately from the backup itself
• Licensing and trusted software binaries if backup software must be reinstalled
• SLA terms with backup vendors for expedited recovery support

For each dependency, confirm the current state: accessible, compromised, or unknown. Do not begin recovery on any system or dependency with an unknown or compromised state — resolve it first.

Enforce Conditional Access policies to block high-risk authentication attempts and require MFA from all users and locations. During an active incident, tightening CA policies limits the damage an attacker can do with stolen credentials.

Immediate high-impact CA policies to apply or verify (Entra ID):
Portal: Entra admin center > Protection > Conditional Access > Policies

1. Block legacy authentication protocols (SMTP, POP, IMAP, basic auth), these bypass MFA entirely. Create a policy: All users > All cloud apps > Client apps: Exchange ActiveSync + Other clients > Block.

2. Require MFA for all users from all locations: no exceptions for on-prem or trusted IPs during an active incident.

3. Block access from non-compliant devices: requires Intune device compliance integration (see ID 50).

For Google Workspace: Admin console > Security > Access and data control > Context-Aware Access > Apply access levels.

WARNING: Enabling MFA-required CA policies will lock out users who have not yet registered an MFA method. Coordinate with the help desk before enabling and ensure the shields-up protocol (ID 66) is active for verification requests.

Combine Intune device compliance policies with Entra ID Conditional Access to automatically quarantine non-compliant devices, blocking their access to corporate resources without waiting for manual investigation of each device.

How it works: Intune marks a device as non-compliant if it fails a compliance check (no EDR, out-of-date OS, missing certificate). Conditional Access then blocks access to cloud apps for non-compliant devices immediately.

Setup steps:
1. Entra admin center > Devices > Compliance policies > Create policy
 Set required conditions: EDR installed and active, OS version minimum, encryption enabled, no jailbreak

2. Entra admin center > Protection > Conditional Access > New policy
 Target: All users > All cloud apps
 Condition: Device compliance: Require device to be marked as compliant
 Grant: Block access if not compliant
 Set non-compliance grace period to 0 hours for immediate effect

WARNING: Devices that are unmanaged (BYOD, contractor devices) will be blocked immediately if they are not enrolled in Intune. Confirm scope with the business before enabling, this may lock out contractors or mobile users. Use 'Report-only' mode first to assess impact if time permits.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 49 (Conditional Access Policies) covers the CA policy layer independently. This action (ID 50) extends ID 49 by adding Intune device compliance as an additional condition.

actions.activity: Firewall QoS Delay (Threat Actor Delay Tactic)
actions.notes:
If C2 or other external channels used by the threat actor have been identified but you haven't found the initial access vector yet, a direct block may tip them off. Using QoS/rate-limiting on the C2 traffic delays the TA's progress while you continue investigating. This tactic is applicable at initial access, lateral movement, and pre-encryption stages, any point before encryption begins where the full access path has not yet been identified and a direct block would risk burning visibility.

WARNING: MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE: Choosing this tactic means active blocking is off the table until the full access path is identified. Do NOT execute alongside ID 7 (DNS Sinkhole), ID 15 (Egress Filtering), ID 37 (Block ALL Traffic), or ID 62 (Block TOR/VPS Ranges). If you block while this is active, the attacker detects the change, abandons the known C2 channel, and pivots to one you have not found yet. Decide before acting: delay and investigate, or block and evict. These are not compatible strategies.

WARNING: ESCALATION THRESHOLD: Reassess this tactic at every stage transition. If the attacker escalates to pre-encryption or begins staging tools, the delay benefit is exhausted, switch to active blocking and eviction immediately. The decision gate is: is the investigative value of maintaining visibility still worth the operational risk of the attacker progressing? If not, execute ID 7, ID 15, or ID 37 and abandon the delay.

actions.activity: SaaS Compromise — Visibility and Log Ingestion
actions.notes:
Establish visibility into SaaS and cloud platform audit logs to determine what the threat actor has accessed, modified, or exfiltrated. During SaaS-targeted incidents, attackers operate entirely within legitimate platform APIs — their activity leaves no trace on endpoints and is only visible in cloud-side audit logs. Without this visibility, containment decisions are made blind.

This action is a prerequisite for informed containment. Complete before or in parallel with ID 53 (Revoke Refresh Tokens) and ID 67 (Revoke OAuth App Registrations) — knowing which accounts and apps the attacker accessed tells you what to revoke and in what order.

Priority log sources to ingest and review:

Microsoft 365 — Unified Audit Log (UAL):
The primary source for all M365-involved incidents. Covers authentication, mail access, file access, admin changes, OAuth consent grants, and cross-service activity. Access via Microsoft Purview Compliance portal > Audit > Search. Filter by the suspected compromise date range and focus on the following activities: UserLoggedIn, MailItemsAccessed, FileAccessed, Add OAuth2PermissionGrant, Add service principal credentials, Set-MailboxAuditing, New-InboxRule.
WARNING: UAL records are retained for 90 days on standard licences and 180 days on E5. Export immediately — records at the retention boundary will be lost.

Entra ID Sign-In Logs:
Review interactive, non-interactive, and service principal sign-in logs for authentication from unexpected IP addresses, unusual user agents, or sign-ins immediately following a user's legitimate authentication (indicating session token theft or AitM relay). Retention default is 30 days — export immediately.

Okta System Log:
If Okta is the identity provider, export the System Log for the suspected compromise window. Focus on user.authentication.sso events, policy.evaluate_sign_on, application.user_membership.add, and any MFA factor lifecycle events. Okta logs are the primary source for detecting MFA device enrollment by an attacker.

Google Workspace Admin Audit and Login Logs:
Review Admin Audit for OAuth app authorisations, delegated admin changes, and Gmail filter or forwarding rule creation. Review Login Reports for suspicious IP addresses or session anomalies.

Salesforce Event Monitoring:
If Salesforce is in scope, review Login History and API Usage logs for bulk data exports, unusual query patterns, or access from unfamiliar IP addresses.

What to look for across all sources:
• Authentication from IP addresses not associated with the legitimate user (especially residential proxies, VPN ranges, or TOR exit nodes)
• Session activity continuing after the legitimate user's session ended
• OAuth application consent grants issued around or after the suspected compromise time
• Mail forwarding rules, inbox rules, or delegation grants not created by the user
• Bulk file access or export events, particularly outside business hours
• Admin role assignments or privilege escalations not matching change records

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 18 (Collect and Preserve TA Logs) covers the export and preservation of these same log sources. This action (ID 52) focuses on the investigative review and interpretation of what the logs show — what did the attacker access, when, and from where. ID 53 (Revoke Refresh Tokens) and ID 67 (Revoke OAuth App Registrations) are the containment actions that follow once the scope of access is understood.

Revoke refresh tokens: invalidate stolen refresh tokens to force attacker sessions to expire and prevent new access token generation.
Disable risky sessions: immediately terminate all active sessions associated with the compromised account or token.

STEPS: PLATFORM-SPECIFIC REVOCATION STEPS:

Okta:
• Revoke all active sessions for compromised users: Admin Console > Directory > People > [User] > More Actions > Clear User Sessions
• Revoke all OAuth tokens: Admin Console > Applications > [App] > Assignments, remove user, then re-add after containment
• Revoke all refresh tokens issued to suspicious apps via Okta Admin > Applications > filter by recent grants

Microsoft Entra ID:
• Revoke all refresh tokens: Revoke-MgUserSignInSession -UserId <UPN> (Graph PowerShell)
• Admin Portal: Entra ID > Users > [User] > Revoke sessions
• For bulk: foreach ($u in $users) { Revoke-MgUserSignInSession -UserId $u.Id }

Google Workspace:
• Admin Console > Users > [User] > Reset sign-in cookies, forces re-authentication
• Revoke all OAuth app authorizations: Admin Console > Security > API controls > App access control
• Remove access for any unrecognised connected apps immediately
• Bulk revocation via Reports API or Admin SDK for all users in the incident scope

WARNING: Token revocation alone is insufficient if rogue OAuth apps remain registered, combine with ID 67 (Audit and Revoke Unauthorised OAuth App Registrations).

WARNING: CRITICAL: Token revocation alone is NOT sufficient:
Revoking user refresh tokens forces session expiry, but malicious OAuth applications registered in your SaaS tenant can silently re-issue new access tokens using their own client credentials, independent of the user's session. Token revocation (this action) and OAuth app revocation (ID 67) must be executed together as a single atomic containment action. Executing one without the other leaves a re-entry path open.

NOTE: SEE ALSO:
• ID 67 (Audit and Revoke Unauthorised OAuth App Registrations): The essential companion to this action for vishing/SaaS identity compromise scenarios.

Enable Microsoft Defender Attack Surface Reduction rules to block common attacker techniques at the execution layer. ASR rules enforce specific behaviour-based blocks independently of signature-based detection — they block the technique regardless of the file or tool used to perform it.

Rules to enable as a priority during incident response:
• Block credential stealing from LSASS: prevents credential dumping tools from reading the Windows Local Security Authority process
• Block persistence via WMI event subscriptions: prevents attackers from registering WMI-based persistence mechanisms that survive reboots
• Block process creation from PSExec and WMI commands: prevents lateral movement via these common propagation channels
• Block execution of potentially obfuscated scripts: targets encoded or obfuscated PowerShell used for payload delivery
• Block executable files that do not meet prevalence, age, or trusted list criteria: blocks newly dropped binaries not previously seen in the environment
• Block abuse of exploited vulnerable signed drivers: prevents driver-based privilege escalation and security tool bypass

Enable via Group Policy: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Microsoft Defender Antivirus > Microsoft Defender Exploit Guard > Attack Surface Reduction. Each rule is identified by a GUID — consult Microsoft's current ASR documentation for GUIDs and recommended states.

Enable in audit mode first to identify legitimate processes that would be blocked before switching to enforce mode. Audit events appear as Event ID 1122 in the Microsoft-Windows-Windows Defender/Operational log.

actions.activity: Implement URL Filtering — Remote Management Tools
actions.notes:
Block the domains and download URLs of remote management tools at the web proxy or DNS filtering layer. Ransomware operators routinely deploy commercial RMM tools for persistence and remote access because they are signed, trusted, and rarely blocked by endpoint controls.

Priority tools to block:
Atera, AnyDesk, Action1, Splashtop, TeamViewer, VNC, RemoteUtilities, ConnectWise, LogMeIn, Supremo, Fleetdeck, NinjaRMM, Mesh Central.

For an extended and regularly updated list of RMM tools observed in ransomware incidents, refer to the Ransomware Tool Matrix:
https://github.com/BushidoUK/Ransomware-Tool-Matrix/blob/main/Tools/RMM-Tools.md

Implementation:
• Web proxy or secure web gateway: add tool domains and download URLs to a custom block category
• DNS filtering platform: create block rules for each tool's primary domains
• Perimeter firewall: block outbound connections to known IP ranges for each tool's relay infrastructure

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 9 (Block Executables via AV/EDR) blocks these tools at the execution layer. ID 7 (DNS Sinkhole) redirects their callback domains. Use all three together for layered coverage.

actions.activity: Check for Spikes in Outbound Traffic
actions.notes:
Review network flow data and proxy logs for abnormal spikes in outbound traffic volume to identify active data exfiltration. Ransomware operators typically exfiltrate data before encrypting — catching an active exfiltration allows the transfer to be blocked or rate-limited before it completes.

Where to look:
• Perimeter firewall flow logs: sort outbound sessions by bytes transferred; focus on sessions to unfamiliar external IPs or cloud storage endpoints with unusually high volumes
• Web proxy logs: filter for large POST or PUT requests to external destinations, particularly cloud storage services (mega.nz, pcloud.com, gofile.io, transfer.sh) or file transfer utilities
• SIEM: alert on outbound data volumes exceeding a baseline threshold per host per hour
• EDR: review processes with high disk read activity combined with active outbound network connections — an indicator of active staging and transfer

Indicators to investigate:
• A single host generating significantly more outbound traffic than its peers
• Large transfers to IP addresses in unusual geographic regions
• Sustained outbound sessions over non-standard ports
• File transfer tool processes (rclone.exe, winscp.exe, FileZilla.exe) with active network sessions

When active exfiltration is identified, apply a firewall block or rate-limit at the perimeter for the destination IP or domain, and isolate the source host (ID 4). Coordinate with ID 51 (QoS Delay) if preserving visibility before blocking is a priority.

Restrict or shut down VPN and streaming technologies such as Citrix and VDI platforms to prevent their use as attacker footholds or initial access vectors. These technologies allow remote connectivity into the environment and should be limited to essential personnel who have reset their credentials and re-registered their MFA method. Non-critical users should have access revoked until the incident is contained.

Access restrictions to apply immediately:
• Limit access to dedicated security and IT personnel only
• Require that any user retaining access has reset their password and re-registered their MFA method before reconnecting
• Terminate all active sessions for users not on the approved list
• Enforce Host Integrity Posture checks where possible: validate that connecting devices have an active EDR agent, are domain-joined, and meet OS patch level requirements
• Revoke access for all non-critical end users until the incident is contained

If the platform is confirmed or strongly suspected as the initial access vector:
• Escalate to full shutdown
• Review platform logs for authentication from unusual source IPs, failed authentication spikes, and sessions initiated around the suspected intrusion time
• Check the deployed version against the vendor's security bulletin history for unpatched CVEs

WARNING: Full shutdown of a streaming platform causes significant business disruption. Apply restriction first and escalate to shutdown only when the platform is confirmed as the entry point or when restriction alone is insufficient.

NOTE: Source: Mandiant Ransomware Protection and Containment Strategies.

SMBv1 is a mass propagation vector still exploited via EternalBlue and similar techniques. Before disabling, check whether SMBv1 is actively in use to avoid accidental business disruption.

Check for active SMBv1 usage first:
Enable SMBv1 audit logging on the server to capture Event ID 3000 in the Microsoft-Windows-SMBServer/Audit event log. Event ID 3000 records every inbound SMBv1 connection attempt. If no 3000 events appear within a monitoring window of 30 to 60 minutes during normal business hours, SMBv1 is not in active use and it is safe to disable without disruption. If 3000 events appear, identify the source hosts and assess whether those connections can be migrated before disabling.

Enable SMBv1 audit logging via Group Policy:
Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > MS Security Guide > Configure SMBv1 Server > Enabled: configure to Audit mode

Or enable via PowerShell on individual servers:
Set-SmbServerConfiguration -AuditSmb1Access $true

Disable once confirmed safe:

Registry, Server (listener):
HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters
SMB1 = REG_DWORD 0

Registry, Client:
HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\services\mrxsmb10
Start = REG_DWORD 4

Group Policy (Microsoft Security Guide template):
Computer Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > MS Security Guide > Configure SMBv1 Server

WARNING: Even if Event ID 3000 shows no active use, legacy devices such as older printers, NAS units, and unmanaged network appliances may use SMBv1 intermittently or only during specific operations. If such devices exist in the environment, verify them individually before applying the disable at scale.

actions.activity: Block Outbound Connections from Specific Windows Binaries
actions.notes:
Attackers use legitimate Windows binaries to download encryptors and backdoors from internal staging points and external C2. Create Windows Firewall outbound block rules for high-risk binaries.

High-priority binaries to block outbound:
• powershell.exe
• bitsadmin.exe
• certutil.exe
• wscript.exe
• cscript.exe
• mshta.exe
• regsvr32.exe

Group Policy path:
Computer Configuration > Policies > Windows Settings > Security Settings > Windows Firewall with Advanced Security > Outbound Rules

Create rule: Program = [binary path], Action = Block, Direction = Outbound. The same rule can be created via PowerShell using New-NetFirewallRule — consult current Microsoft documentation for syntax.

WARNING: Test in audit/monitor mode first. Blocking PowerShell outbound may impact legitimate monitoring agents and management tools. Scope to workstations first before servers.

actions.activity: Isolate and Restrict Access to PAM / Password Vault Systems
actions.notes:
If the attacker reaches your PAM system or password vault, all credential rotation efforts become worthless, they can simply re-check out credentials. Isolate and lock down the vault immediately.

WARNING: EXECUTE BEFORE ID 13 and ID 29, This action must be completed before any credential rotation. Rotating passwords into a compromised vault gives the attacker the new credentials.

Containment steps:
1. Restrict network access to PAM/vault system to a small set of trusted IPs or a dedicated PAW segment only
2. Require MFA re-authentication for all vault access: revoke any active sessions
3. Do NOT store newly rotated passwords in the vault until the incident is fully contained
4. Decouple PAM system authentication from the compromised Active Directory if AD integration is in place, switch to local PAM accounts
5. Reduce the scope of accounts with vault access to the minimum required for the response
6. Enable enhanced logging on all PAM authentication and credential checkout events
7. Review recent credential checkout history for suspicious access patterns

Common PAM platforms include enterprise password vault and privileged access management solutions

WARNING: Do not disable the PAM system entirely, responders need it for managing the response. The goal is isolation and session eviction, not shutdown.

actions.activity: Lock External Self-Service Password Reset (SSPR) and Harden Help Desk Verification
actions.notes:
Social engineering of the help desk and abuse of self-service password reset is the primary initial access vector for Scattered Spider and increasingly used by other groups. This is the emergency version of ID 32, use when social engineering or SSPR abuse is confirmed or actively suspected.

Immediate actions:
1. Temporarily disable external self-service password reset (SSPR), require users to contact IT directly
2. For Microsoft Entra ID: Disable SSPR from external locations in Authentication Methods > Password Reset > Properties
3. Require all password reset and MFA device modification requests to go through an enhanced verification workflow:
 • On-camera or in-person identity verification
 • Challenge/response questions based on internal-only knowledge (not DOB or last 4 SSN, attackers have this)
 • Out-of-band callback to a registered corporate number
4. Restrict MFA device re-registration to corporate locations or trusted IP ranges only
5. Notify help desk staff of the active incident and instruct them not to action any account modification requests without escalation
6. Alert on any self-service password reset or MFA registration events during the incident window

WARNING: Communicate to end users via out-of-band channel (email, Teams, SMS blast) that password resets must go through IT directly. Without this communication, help desk call volume will spike.
WARNING: See also ID 32 (Harden Password Reset Processes), the measured version used earlier in the investigation before escalation to this action.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: Human/procedural complement:
• ID 66 (Activate Help Desk Shields-Up Protocol): This action (ID 61) covers the TECHNICAL layer, disabling SSPR portals and restricting MFA registration in the IdP. ID 66 covers the HUMAN/PROCEDURAL layer, briefing service desk staff on heightened verification requirements. Both must be executed in the same window. If the technical lock (ID 61) is active but the service desk isn't briefed (ID 66), attackers will pivot to calling the help desk directly to request manual resets, bypassing the technical controls entirely.

actions.activity: Block TOR Exit Nodes and Known VPS / Bulletproof Hosting IP Ranges
actions.notes:
C2 traffic, exfiltration, and attacker remote access frequently routes through TOR exit nodes and bulletproof VPS ranges. Blocking these at the perimeter cuts interactive attacker access and slows or stops active exfiltration with minimal legitimate business impact.

Immediate steps:
1. Block all known TOR exit node IP ranges at the perimeter firewall and proxy
 • Maintained list: https://check.torproject.org/torbulkexitlist
 • Your threat intelligence feed or service (if subscribed) will maintain an updated TOR exit node list
2. Block outbound traffic to known VPS/bulletproof hosting ASNs commonly used by ransomware operators:
 • Common ASNs: AS9009 (M247), AS60781 (LeaseWeb), AS208091, AS59900, check your threat intel feed for current list
3. Block outbound to IP ranges not associated with known CDNs, SaaS platforms, or business-critical destinations (allowlist approach for servers if feasible)
4. If a SIEM or proxy is available, alert on any successful connections to TOR or known malicious IP ranges before the block is in place

WARNING: TOR exit lists change frequently. Use a threat intel feed rather than a static list for ongoing coverage. Some legitimate privacy tools and research functions use TOR, in an active incident, block first and carve exceptions later.

actions.activity: Hunt for and Restrict Rogue VMs, Bastion Hosts, and Newly Joined Devices
actions.notes:
Attackers create rogue virtual machines, bastion hosts, or join attacker-controlled devices to the domain to establish persistent footholds that survive credential rotation and host remediation.

Hunting steps:
1. Review vCenter / ESXi for virtual machines created or modified in the last 30 days, compare against change management records
2. Review Active Directory for devices joined to the domain in the last 30 days using Get-ADComputer filtered by WhenCreated date, or by checking the Computers container in Active Directory Users and Computers sorted by creation date.
3. Review Entra ID / Azure AD for newly registered or joined devices
4. Check for newly created or modified network security group rules and publicly exposed resources in cloud environments
5. Alert on new programmatic access keys (AWS Access Keys, Azure Service Principals, GCP Service Accounts) created during the incident window
6. Review authentication logs for device names using default Windows naming conventions (DESKTOP-XXXXXXX, WIN-XXXXXXX), common indicator of attacker-staged devices

 SAAS TENANT, HUNT FOR ROGUE OAUTH APP REGISTRATIONS:
For SaaS-targeted attacks (vishing, MFA manipulation, ShinyHunters-type), attackers register malicious OAuth applications to maintain persistent access and manipulate mailboxes silently. Hunt these alongside device/VM hunting:
7. Google Workspace: Admin Console > Security > API controls > App access control, review all third-party apps with data access granted in the last 30 days; revoke any unrecognised app immediately
8. Microsoft Entra ID: Entra admin center > Applications > Enterprise applications > filter by Created date, review all apps registered or consented to recently; remove any unauthorised registrations
9. Okta: Admin Console > Applications: filter by Created date; review all apps added during the incident window and revoke any not in the approved inventory
10. Salesforce: Setup > Connected Apps OAuth Usage: review all connected apps and revoke any not approved
• Treat any unrecognised OAuth app with mail, files, or admin scopes as a confirmed persistence mechanism, revoke immediately and treat the granting account as compromised
• See ID 67 (Audit and Revoke Unauthorised OAuth App Registrations) for the dedicated SaaS OAuth action

Containment:
• Disable or remove any unrecognised devices from the domain immediately
• Power off and snapshot any unrecognised VMs before removal for forensic review
• Revoke any programmatic keys created during the incident window

WARNING: Coordinate with IT operations before removing devices, confirm each device is unrecognised and not a legitimate recent deployment.

Disable the compromised IAM user to revoke long-term access key usage and console access.

WARNING: CRITICAL: Disabling an IAM user does NOT terminate active AWS sessions already in progress. Any session tokens already issued (via AWS STS AssumeRole) remain valid until they naturally expire (up to 12 hours). Disabling the user is necessary but not sufficient. You must also attach an explicit deny policy to kill in-flight sessions.

Containment actions (consult AWS documentation or IAM console for current steps):
1. Disable console access and deactivate all access keys for the compromised user
2. Attach an explicit deny-all policy to the user to terminate in-flight sessions immediately
3. If credentials originated from AssumeRole via STS, the compromised principal is the role, not the user. Take all of the following actions against the role:
   • Remove the role trust policy to block new federation tokens
   • Detach all permissions from the role
   • Attach an explicit deny-all inline policy to the role
4. Review IAM for any attacker-registered MFA devices and remove them
5. Review CloudTrail events for the compromised username to identify lateral movement or persistence actions taken during the compromise window

WARNING: Do NOT delete the IAM user or role during the incident, deletion destroys the audit trail. Disable and deny only.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 65 (Block Compromised AWS Federated User) covers the separate scenario where the identity is federated via Okta, Entra ID, or ADFS and there is no persistent IAM user to disable. Determine which applies before acting, applying IAM user disable to a federated identity will have no effect.

Federated identities access AWS via an external IdP (Okta, Entra ID, ADFS) using SAML or OIDC. There is no persistent IAM user to disable — containment must happen at the IdP AND in AWS simultaneously.

The same STS session persistence risk applies as described in ID 64 — blocking the identity at the IdP does NOT immediately terminate existing AWS sessions. SAML/OIDC-issued STS credentials remain valid until they expire (typically 1 to 12 hours). Attaching an explicit deny policy to the IAM role is required to kill in-flight sessions immediately. Never rely on the IdP block alone.

Containment steps (execute simultaneously):
1. Disable or suspend the user at the IdP (Okta: suspend user / Entra ID: block sign-in / ADFS: disable AD account)
2. Revoke all active sessions and refresh tokens at the IdP (see ID 53)
3. Identify the IAM role used via CloudTrail: look for AssumeRoleWithSAML or AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity events
4. Attach an explicit deny-all policy to that IAM role to kill in-flight sessions immediately
5. Remove the role trust policy to block new federation tokens from being issued
6. Review CloudTrail for any lateral movement or persistence actions taken from the compromised session

WARNING: If the IAM role is shared across multiple users, scope the deny using sts:RoleSessionName condition to limit blast radius to the compromised session only.

As with ID 64, do not delete the IAM role — this destroys the audit trail. Disable and deny only.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 64 (Block Compromised AWS IAM User) covers the scenario where the identity has a persistent IAM user (long-term access keys, console password). Determine which applies to your environment before acting — the correct action depends entirely on whether the identity is federated or native IAM.

During an active incident involving vishing or social engineering, the service desk is a direct attack surface. Threat actors impersonate employees and third-party vendors to manipulate help desk staff into resetting MFA or granting access. Activate shields-up procedures immediately.

Immediate actions for the service desk:
1. Require live video call for ALL account modification requests, user must hold a physical government ID next to their face; agent must visually verify the match
2. Confirm the name on the ID matches the employee's corporate record
3. Require out-of-band manager approval before processing any password reset or MFA change, contact the manager at a known corporate number, not the one provided by the caller
4. NEVER process a reset based solely on employee ID, SSN, date of birth, or manager name, attackers obtain this data from prior breaches
5. Always call back at a known-good corporate number before performing any reset, never trust the inbound caller's number (spoofing is trivial)
6. If live video is not possible, require the user to come in person. No exceptions for high-privileged accounts
7. End any inbound call from a third-party vendor claiming to need access, independently verify via the vendor's known account manager using on-file contact info only

Communications:
• Brief ALL service desk staff on the active incident and heightened procedures, do not wait for a formal communication chain
• Notify technology operations staff not to accept work direction via SMS from colleagues during the incident window
• Communicate to end users (via out-of-band channel) that MFA and password changes must go through IT directly, not self-service
• Alert HR and business unit managers to watch for suspicious account activity requests from staff

WARNING: This is the single most effective containment action against vishing-based attacks. Social engineering bypasses all technical controls, the service desk verification process is the last line of defence.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: Technical complement:
• ID 61 (Lock External SSPR and Harden Help Desk Verification): This action (ID 66) covers the HUMAN/PROCEDURAL layer, briefing staff and activating verification protocols. ID 61 covers the TECHNICAL layer, disabling self-service password reset portals and restricting MFA registration in the IdP. Both must be activated in the same window. Locking the portal (ID 61) without briefing the service desk (ID 66) means attackers simply call the help desk instead. Activating the service desk protocol (ID 66) without the technical lock (ID 61) leaves the SSPR portal open as an alternative attack path.

actions.activity: Audit and Revoke Unauthorised OAuth App Registrations
actions.notes:
Attackers register malicious OAuth applications in victim SaaS tenants to maintain persistent access that survives session revocation and MFA changes. OAuth app registrations grant long-lived access to mailboxes, files, and data using delegated permissions, they are invisible to the compromised user and survive password resets unless explicitly revoked.

Audit and revoke across all SaaS platforms:

Google Workspace:
• Admin Console > Security > API controls > App access control > Manage Third-Party App Access
• Review all apps with granted scopes: focus on mail, drive, and admin scopes
• Remove any app not in your approved inventory: revoke immediately, do not wait to confirm malicious intent
• Block new third-party app authorisations: Admin Console > Security > API controls > set to "Don't allow users to access any third-party apps"
• Specifically hunt for apps with Gmail delete/modify scope, the ShinyHunters ToogleBox app used this to delete security notification emails

Microsoft Entra ID:
• Entra admin center > Applications > Enterprise applications > filter by Created date
• Review all apps consented to in the last 30: 90 days, compare against approved app inventory
• Revoke user consent for suspicious apps: remove app from Enterprise Applications
• Restrict future consent: Entra admin center > Enterprise applications > Consent and permissions > set User consent to "Do not allow user consent"
• Require admin approval for all application registrations going forward

Okta:
• Admin Console > Applications: review all apps sorted by Created date
• Revoke any unrecognised application or app with suspicious OAuth scopes
• Review all API tokens: Admin Console > Security > API > Tokens, revoke any unknown token

Salesforce:
• Setup > Connected Apps OAuth Usage: review all connected apps and their access counts
• Revoke access for any unrecognised connected app
• Setup > OAuth and OpenID Connect Settings: review and restrict external OAuth usage

WARNING: Revoking the user's session tokens (ID 53) does NOT revoke OAuth app authorisations. These must be revoked separately and explicitly.
WARNING: Treat any unrecognised OAuth app with mail, files, calendar, or admin scopes as a confirmed persistence mechanism, revoke and investigate the granting account.

WARNING: SEQUENCING DEPENDENCY: Before revoking OAuth app registrations, confirm:
• ID 53 (Revoke Refresh Tokens): Revoke all user session tokens first so the attacker loses their authentication context. Revoking the OAuth app without revoking tokens leaves active sessions running. Execute ID 53 and ID 67 in the same containment window, treat them as a single atomic action.

NOTE: SEE ALSO:
• ID 33 (Pause MFA Enrollment): OAuth apps can provide authentication context bypassing MFA, pause MFA enrollment (ID 33) alongside revoking OAuth apps to close both vectors simultaneously.
• ID 63 (Hunt Rogue VMs/Devices): Includes the discovery/hunting steps for OAuth app registrations (steps 7, 10). Use ID 63 to identify which apps to revoke, then execute this action (ID 67) to revoke them.

Attackers in SaaS-targeted incidents (ShinyHunters, Scattered Spider) exfiltrate data entirely through native SaaS export features rather than external tools, making this invisible to traditional DLP and outbound traffic monitoring. Target: Google Takeout, Salesforce Data Loader, SharePoint/OneDrive bulk download via PowerShell, Atlassian bulk exports, and DocuSign bulk envelope downloads.

Google Workspace, Disable Google Takeout:
• Admin Console > Account > Account settings > Data export, disable for non-admin users
• Admin Console > Users > Data export: confirm bulk export is disabled org-wide
• Alert on any Takeout export initiation event in the Audit log during the incident window

Microsoft 365, Restrict SharePoint/OneDrive bulk download:
• SharePoint Admin Center > Policies > Access control > set "Unmanaged devices" to allow view-only (no download)
• Block PowerShell-based access: SharePoint Admin Center > Access control > Legacy authentication, disable to prevent scripted access
• Conditional Access: require compliant device for SharePoint/OneDrive access, blocks attacker downloading from their own machine with stolen tokens
• Alert on FileDownloaded events with WindowsPowerShell user-agent string via M365 Purview audit logs

Salesforce, Restrict Data Loader and report exports:
• Setup > Data Export: restrict or disable scheduled and on-demand data exports for non-admin users
• Setup > Reports and Dashboards > Report Settings: disable "Allow export" for standard users during the incident
• Setup > Connected Apps: revoke access for Salesforce Data Loader if it is being abused
• Monitor BulkApiResultEvent and ReportEventStream for any activity during the incident window

Atlassian (Confluence / Jira):
• Space settings > Permissions: restrict export permissions for spaces containing sensitive data
• Organisation admin: disable bulk page/space export for non-admin users during the incident
• Alert on any bulk export or large-volume page access events in the audit log

DocuSign:
• Organisation admin > Security > Session management: force session termination for compromised accounts
• Monitor BulkSend and envelope bulk-download events in DocuSign Monitor audit logs
• Restrict API token access: revoke any API integration key created during the incident window

WARNING: Restricting exports will impact legitimate business operations. Communicate to affected users and define a temporary exception process for business-critical export needs.
WARNING: Combine with ID 67 (Revoke OAuth App Registrations), attackers may have OAuth apps with files.read scope that bypass direct export restrictions.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: Execute in the same containment window:
• ID 53 (Revoke Refresh Tokens): Restricting bulk exports while the attacker still holds valid session tokens means they can still download data via authenticated API calls before token expiry. Revoke tokens (ID 53) and restrict exports (ID 68) simultaneously.
• ID 67 (Revoke OAuth App Registrations): An attacker with a malicious OAuth app can trigger bulk exports using the app's own client credentials, bypassing user token revocation entirely. Revoking the OAuth app (ID 67) closes this path. Execute IDs 53, 67, and 68 as a single coordinated action for SaaS exfil containment.

actions.activity: Scan for and Remediate Exposed Credentials in Code and Config
actions.notes:
Attackers targeting SaaS environments (ShinyHunters, Scattered Spider) actively exploit long-lived credentials embedded in source code, CI/CD pipelines, and configuration files. These non-human identities often carry privileged access and are rarely rotated, once found, they provide persistent access that survives session revocation, MFA changes, and even full password resets for human accounts.

Immediate scanning steps:
1. Source code repositories (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, Azure DevOps):
 • Enable native "Push Protection" or "Secret Scanning", blocks new commits containing credentials before merge
 • Run a historical scan to identify secrets already committed using your repository's native secret scanning feature or an open source secret scanning tool
 • Scan ALL branches and commit history, not just the default branch
 • Revoke any discovered credential immediately before it can be exploited if not already

2. CI/CD pipeline secrets (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, Azure Pipelines):
 • Audit all pipeline variables and secrets stores, identify any credentials that are broadly accessible across pipelines
 • Rotate all secrets found in pipeline configs, treat all as compromised if the repo was accessible to the attacker
 • Replace static credentials with short-lived tokens via workload identity federation (OIDC) where feasible

3. Cloud configuration files and IaC (Terraform, CloudFormation, Helm charts):
 • Scan for hard-coded access keys, passwords, or tokens in IaC templates
 • Common patterns: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID, client_secret, password=, api_key=, private_key

4. Containerised environments:
 • Scan container images and Dockerfiles for embedded credentials
 • Review environment variables in container definitions and Kubernetes secrets

Post-discovery actions:
• Rotate ALL discovered credentials immediately: do not attempt to assess whether they were accessed first
• Transition long-lived static credentials to workload identity federation / managed identities where feasible
• Enforce strict scoping: credentials should be bound to specific resources, not granted broad permissions
• Restrict authentication IPs for programmatic credentials to trusted IP ranges
• Baseline expected behaviour for each credential type and integrate anomaly alerting into the SIEM

WARNING: Non-human identities are frequently assigned privileged roles with minimal monitoring, assume any discovered credential has been used by the attacker until proven otherwise.

WARNING: SEQUENCING NOTE: Credential rotation timing:
Ideally, secret scanning (this action) should complete before or concurrently with credential rotation (ID 13, ID 29). If credentials embedded in source code or CI/CD pipelines are not discovered and removed first, developers may commit the newly rotated credentials into the same repositories during normal work, immediately re-exposing them. Run scanning tools before declaring credential rotation complete, and re-scan after rotation to confirm no new leaks.

NOTE: SEE ALSO:
• IDs 64/65 (Block AWS IAM/Federated User): Those actions remediate specific known-compromised identities. This action (ID 69) is a broader sweep that may reveal additional embedded credentials, including AWS access keys, that were not part of the original identified compromise. Run ID 69 alongside or after IDs 64/65 to ensure full coverage.

Disabling confirmed or suspected compromised user accounts immediately revokes their authentication path across all integrated systems. Unlike credential rotation, which replaces a password the attacker may have already exfiltrated, disabling an account blocks all further authentication attempts regardless of what credentials the attacker holds. This is one of the most direct and high-certainty containment actions available at any stage of the incident.

LATERAL MOVEMENT CONTEXT: During lateral movement, the attacker is pivoting between systems using valid user credentials. Disabling the specific accounts observed in attacker activity logs (EDR telemetry, authentication logs, SIEM) directly cuts the pivot path. Unlike network isolation (ID 4), which stops all traffic to a host, account disable surgically removes the identity without affecting other users on the same system. Prioritise disabling accounts observed authenticating from attacker-controlled hosts or from anomalous source IPs before those accounts are used to establish persistence via new accounts, GPO modifications, or scheduled tasks.

This action targets standard human user accounts. For privileged accounts (Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins), see ID 13 (Rotate All Privileged Accounts). For service accounts, see ID 11. For AWS IAM and federated identities, see IDs 64 and 65.

Accounts to prioritise:
• Any account confirmed as the initial access vector (phished, purchased, or brute-forced)
• Any account observed in attacker activity logs that does not belong to a confirmed responder
• Any account that authenticated from an attacker-controlled IP or during anomalous hours
• Accounts belonging to departed employees or contractors that remain active
• Any account flagged as high-risk by your IdP identity protection or UEBA tooling

How to disable by platform:

Active Directory:
Disable the account in Active Directory Users and Computers (ADUC) or via the Active Directory Administrative Center. Right-click the account and select Disable. For bulk disabling, use the Disable-ADAccount cmdlet in PowerShell or a scripted approach via your IT automation tooling.

Microsoft Entra ID (cloud-only or hybrid):
Entra admin center > Users > [User] > Properties > Block sign-in: Yes. This immediately prevents new authentications. For hybrid accounts, disable at the on-premises AD source — the disabled state will sync to Entra ID within the next sync cycle (typically 30 minutes). If an immediate cloud-side block is required before sync completes, block sign-in in Entra ID directly AND disable in on-prem AD.

Okta:
Admin Console > Directory > People > [User] > More Actions > Suspend (preserves the account and audit trail, reversible) or Deactivate (permanent, removes from all app assignments). During an active incident, prefer Suspend to retain the forensic record and allow reinstatement after investigation.

Google Workspace:
Admin Console > Users > [User] > Suspend user. Suspending immediately blocks sign-in and terminates active sessions. The account and its data are preserved.

WARNING: Disabling an account does NOT immediately terminate sessions already authenticated. Active tokens and session cookies issued before the disable remain valid until they expire or are explicitly revoked. After disabling, always revoke active sessions at the IdP level. Use the Revoke sessions option in the user profile for Entra ID, Clear User Sessions in Okta, and Reset sign-in cookies in Google Workspace. For SaaS accounts, combine with ID 53 (Revoke Refresh Tokens).

WARNING: For hybrid AD and Entra ID environments, account state conflicts can cause unexpected re-enablement if the disable is applied only at one layer. Apply at both on-premises AD and Entra ID simultaneously and verify sync status before treating the account as fully disabled. If AD-to-Entra sync is still active (see ID 5), a delay of up to 30 minutes exists before the cloud-side block takes effect from an AD-side disable.

WARNING: Before disabling any account, confirm the owner is not an active incident responder or a critical business role required during the response. Maintain a protected list of responder accounts and cross-check it before any bulk disable action.

NOTE: SEE ALSO:
• ID 53 (Revoke Refresh Tokens): Always revoke active sessions alongside account disable, particularly for SaaS and cloud accounts where token-based access survives account state changes.
• ID 13 (Rotate All Privileged Accounts): Covers Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, and all other privileged human accounts. This action (ID 70) covers standard user accounts confirmed as compromised. Run both in parallel.
• ID 11 (Service Accounts): Covers non-human accounts. Run alongside ID 70 when service account compromise is suspected.

Take the backup infrastructure offline to prevent ransomware from reaching and encrypting backup repositories. Ransomware operators specifically target backup systems to destroy the organisation's recovery path — enterprise backup platforms are targeted by name in documented ransomware playbooks. If the backup system is encrypted or corrupted before containment, recovery becomes significantly more difficult or impossible.

Immediate isolation steps:
1. Disconnect the backup server from the production network. Apply a deny-all firewall rule for the backup server's IP or VLAN, or physically remove the network connection. The backup server must not be reachable from any potentially compromised host.
2. Disable backup agents on all confirmed or suspected compromised hosts. Backup agents run with elevated privileges and can be abused as a channel to reach the backup server from inside the compromised environment. Disable the backup agent service on hosts where attacker activity has been observed — consult your backup platform documentation for the service name on each OS.
3. Suspend all active backup jobs immediately. An active backup job running during encryption may back up encrypted files over clean restore points, corrupting the recovery set.
4. Revoke or change the backup service account credentials. Backup service accounts typically hold domain-level read access across all servers — if compromised, they provide the attacker with a read path to every system the backup agent covers. See ID 72 (Rotate Backup Credentials) for the full credential rotation procedure.
5. If cloud backup targets are in use (S3, Azure Blob, Wasabi), apply bucket or container policies to deny delete and overwrite operations. Enable object lock or immutability on the target storage if not already active.

Backup platform service management:
Each backup platform provides a way to take repositories offline and stop its core service. Consult your platform's documentation for the exact steps — the general procedure is: take the backup repository or storage unit offline via the management console, then stop the backup server service so no new connections can be initiated.

WARNING: Do NOT delete or format backup repositories during containment. Destroying backup data removes your recovery options. Isolate and protect the data — do not purge it.

WARNING: Isolating the backup server will stop all backup jobs and may suspend backup monitoring alerts. Notify the backup team and IR lead before executing so they do not mistake the silence for normal operation.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 72 (Rotate Backup Credentials) must be executed alongside or immediately after this action. ID 73 (Verify Backup Integrity and Immutability) confirms whether the backups that survived are usable for recovery. ID 48 (Identify Backup Infrastructure Dependencies) should be completed before any recovery attempt.

Rotate all credentials associated with the backup infrastructure to ensure an attacker who has observed or extracted backup system credentials cannot re-access the backup environment after containment. Backup systems are high-value targets precisely because their service accounts carry broad read access across the environment — the same access that makes backups comprehensive also makes those credentials dangerous if compromised.

Credentials to rotate:

Backup software admin console accounts:
• Rotate the local administrator password for the backup server itself
• Rotate the backup software admin account (the local administrator account for the backup platform console)
• Rotate any web console or REST API credentials for the backup platform

Backup service accounts (used by agents to connect to the backup server):
• Identify the service account the backup agents use to authenticate to the backup server — commonly a domain service account with read access to all protected systems
• Rotate that credential immediately and update the backup server configuration with the new credential
• Update agent configuration on all managed endpoints with the new service account credential

Cloud backup target credentials:
• Rotate or deactivate any API keys, access keys, or SAS tokens used by the backup platform to write to cloud storage (S3 access keys, Azure Storage SAS tokens, GCP service account keys)
• Issue new credentials with the minimum required permissions — write for backup jobs, read for restore jobs, no delete permission

Encryption key passphrase (if applicable):
• If backup data is encrypted with a passphrase managed by the organisation, rotate the passphrase and store the new value out-of-band in hardcopy or a physically isolated secure store
• Do not store the new passphrase in the same PAM system or password manager used for other credentials until containment is confirmed (see ID 60)

WARNING: SEQUENCING DEPENDENCY: Complete ID 71 (Isolate Backup Infrastructure) before rotating credentials. If the backup server is still reachable from compromised hosts when new credentials are issued, the attacker can observe the new credential through the still-live agent connection. Isolate first, rotate second.

WARNING: SEQUENCING DEPENDENCY: Complete ID 60 (Isolate PAM / Password Vault) before rotating backup credentials. Storing new backup credentials in a compromised vault provides no protection.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 71 (Isolate Backup Infrastructure) is the prerequisite action. ID 13 (Rotate All Privileged Accounts) covers domain admin and other privileged accounts — the backup service account may also appear in that sweep. ID 73 (Verify Backup Integrity) should follow once credentials are rotated and the backup environment is secured.

Verify that backup data is intact, predates the compromise, has not been encrypted or corrupted, and can actually be used for recovery. A backup that exists but is encrypted, corrupted, or too recent to predate the initial compromise has no recovery value. This verification must happen before any recovery effort begins and before communicating recovery timelines to the business.

Step 1: Establish the compromise timeline.
Confirm the earliest suspected date of attacker access from EDR telemetry, authentication logs, and threat intelligence. This is the cutoff — backups that postdate this point may contain attacker artefacts, malware, or encrypted files and cannot be trusted for clean recovery.

Step 2: Identify the most recent clean restore point.
Find the most recent backup job that completed before the compromise timeline. For each candidate restore point, confirm:
• The backup job completed successfully with no errors
• The backup catalogue entry shows the expected file counts and sizes — a sudden drop in protected data may indicate the encryptor was already running when the backup ran
• The backup file itself has not been modified since the job completed — compare file hashes or check the backup platform's own integrity logs

Step 3: Verify immutability and air-gap status.
• For cloud backup targets (S3, Azure Blob, Wasabi): confirm that Object Lock or immutability policies are active on the storage bucket or container. Verify that no delete or overwrite operations have been executed on the backup objects since the compromise date by reviewing the cloud provider's access logs (S3 server access logging, Azure Storage diagnostic logs).
• For tape or removable media: confirm physical custody — verify the tape or drive has been offline since the last backup job and has not been connected to any system in the environment during the incident window.
• For deduplicated repositories: run the backup platform's built-in integrity check or data verification job against the candidate restore point. Most enterprise backup platforms have a built-in verify or test-restore function that confirms the backup is internally consistent without performing a full restore — consult your platform's documentation for the current procedure.

Step 4: Perform a test restore on an isolated system.
Before committing to a full production restore, restore a representative set of files or a single non-critical system to an isolated environment. Confirm the restored data:
• Opens and functions correctly
• Does not contain encrypted file extensions introduced by the ransomware
• Does not trigger EDR or AV alerts on the restored host

Step 5: Document the verified recovery baseline.
Record the restore point date and time, the systems covered, the backup platform and job name, and the integrity verification method used. This becomes the authoritative recovery baseline for the incident.

WARNING: Do not begin any production recovery until a clean restore point has been verified. Restoring from a backup that itself contains the ransomware or the attacker's persistence mechanism will restart the incident.

WARNING: VSS (Volume Shadow Copies) on Windows endpoints are typically deleted by ransomware before or during encryption. Do not rely on VSS as a primary recovery source — verify dedicated backup copies first. If VSS copies are found intact, treat them as supplemental to a verified backup, not a substitute.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 48 (Identify Backup Infrastructure Dependencies) maps the backup environment and must be completed before this action. ID 71 (Isolate Backup Infrastructure) must be completed to ensure the backup environment is stable before verification begins.

Terminate the identified malicious process to stop active encryption or lateral movement immediately, without taking the entire host off the network. This is the most surgical containment action available when the specific malicious process has been identified and you need to stop its activity while preserving the host for forensics and continued investigation.

Use when:
• Active encryption is in progress and you can identify the encryptor process by name, PID, or file hash in the EDR console or Task Manager
• A known attacker tool (C2 beacon, lateral movement binary, credential dumper) is running and you want to terminate it without isolating the host
• You need to buy time to complete forensic collection before isolating the host (see ID 4)

Methods:

Via EDR console:
Most EDR platforms support remote process termination from the management console — locate the process by name or PID on the target host and issue a terminate or kill command. This is the fastest and least disruptive method.

Via Task Manager (local or remote):
Open Task Manager > Details tab > right-click the process > End Process Tree. Use End Process Tree rather than End Process to also terminate any child processes spawned by the malicious parent.

Via PowerShell (if WinRM or PSRemoting is available):
Stop-Process -Name <processname> -Force
Or by PID: Stop-Process -Id <PID> -Force

Via PsKill or similar tools (on host or via admin share):
pskill \\<hostname> <PID or processname>

WARNING: Killing a process does not guarantee the attacker cannot restart it. The binary may be scheduled, service-backed, or re-launched by a watchdog process. After terminating the process, immediately proceed to host isolation (ID 4) or firewall blocking (ID 16/38) to prevent re-launch from attacker-controlled infrastructure.

WARNING: Killing an active encryption process does NOT decrypt already-encrypted files. It stops further encryption from that process only. Other processes or hosts may still be encrypting concurrently.

WARNING: Do not kill the process before capturing a memory dump or forensic image if volatile evidence is needed. The process termination destroys in-memory artefacts including encryption keys that could be used for decryption. Capture first if forensics allow the time.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 4 (Isolate Host) is the next action after process termination — killing the process buys time, isolation removes the host from the attacker's reach. ID 16 and ID 38 (Firewall Rules) can prevent process re-launch from network-based execution paths.

During active encryption and post-encryption, the primary corporate communications infrastructure — email, Teams, Slack, VoIP, ticketing systems — must be treated as potentially compromised, monitored by the attacker, or unavailable due to encryption. Establishing a dedicated out-of-band channel before attempting any coordinated containment action ensures that attacker-held access to corporate comms does not expose your response strategy, and that the team can coordinate even if internal systems are fully offline.

Establish immediately on detection of active or post-encryption events:

1. Designate a primary out-of-band channel:
Use a platform entirely outside the corporate environment — personal mobile devices with a consumer messaging app (Signal is recommended for end-to-end encryption), a pre-provisioned external conference bridge, or an IR retainer's secure portal. The channel must not depend on corporate identity providers, corporate SSO, corporate email, or any system on the affected network.

2. Establish a secondary backup channel:
Designate a fallback method in case the primary is unreachable — typically direct mobile phone calls between key responders using pre-shared personal numbers. Maintain a printed or offline contact list for all IR team members, legal counsel, the IR retainer, and executive stakeholders.

3. Define who is on the channel:
Limit membership to active responders — IR lead, technical leads, legal counsel, and executive sponsor. Do not include the broader IT team or business units on the primary tactical channel. Keep the size manageable; create a separate stakeholder update channel if leadership requires regular briefings.

4. Establish communication discipline:
All containment decisions, action approvals, and response activities must be communicated and documented on the out-of-band channel — not over corporate email or Teams. Assume the attacker can read anything sent over corporate infrastructure until full eradication is confirmed.

5. Notify staff via out-of-band means:
If internal communications systems are unavailable or must be shut down as part of containment, notify employees of system unavailability and expected timelines via personal email or SMS rather than corporate channels. Prepare a brief, non-technical message that does not disclose the nature of the incident.

WARNING: Do not use any communication platform that authenticates via the compromised corporate identity provider. If the attacker holds admin access to Entra ID, Microsoft 365, or Google Workspace, they may be able to read messages sent through those platforms or intercept authentication.

WARNING: Avoid discussing specific containment actions, identified attacker tools, or the scope of compromise over any channel that has not been confirmed as out-of-band. Premature disclosure of containment intent over a monitored channel may cause the attacker to accelerate encryption, destroy evidence, or exfiltrate additional data before actions can be executed.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 37 (Block ALL Traffic) and ID 36 (Restrict VPN) may take down the communication infrastructure responders are relying on — confirm the out-of-band channel is operational before executing either of those actions.

Ransomware operators and pre-ransomware actors use Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver (BYOVD) attacks to load a legitimately signed but exploitable kernel driver, escalate to kernel-level privileges, and then disable or kill endpoint security software (EDR, AV) before deploying the encryptor. Blocking known vulnerable drivers prevents this technique from succeeding even if the attacker has user-level code execution.

Microsoft maintains an official Vulnerable Driver Blocklist that is enforced by Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) and Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity (HVCI). The blocklist is updated as new vulnerable drivers are discovered and is available at: https://aka.ms/VulnerableDriverBlockList

Method 1 — Enable HVCI (Hypervisor-Protected Code Integrity):
HVCI uses hardware virtualisation to enforce kernel memory protections and applies the Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist automatically. This is the most effective method but requires compatible hardware and a reboot.
Enable via Group Policy:
Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > Device Guard > Turn On Virtualization Based Security > Enabled
Set 'Virtualization Based Protection of Code Integrity' to Enabled with UEFI lock.
Or via Windows Security: Device Security > Core Isolation > Memory Integrity > On.
WARNING: HVCI requires a reboot to take effect. Do not reboot compromised hosts before forensic collection is complete. Apply to clean, non-compromised hosts during the incident window.

Method 2 — Deploy the Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist via WDAC policy:
For environments that cannot enable HVCI immediately, the blocklist can be applied as a standalone WDAC supplemental policy.
Download the current blocklist policy from: https://aka.ms/VulnerableDriverBlockList
Deploy the policy via Group Policy or MDM (Intune):
GPO path: Computer Configuration > Administrative Templates > System > Device Guard > Deploy Windows Defender Application Control
The blocklist blocks driver files by hash and by publisher certificate where the entire publisher is blocked.

Method 3 — AV/EDR block rules for known LOLDrivers:
Most enterprise AV and EDR platforms maintain a set of block rules for known vulnerable and abused drivers. Enable or verify that vulnerable driver blocking is active in your AV/EDR policy console. Common drivers abused in BYOVD attacks include but are not limited to RTCore64.sys, mhyprot2.sys, DBUtil_2_3.sys, and gdrv.sys — the full current list is maintained at https://aka.ms/VulnerableDriverBlockList and by the LOLDrivers project at https://www.loldrivers.io

WARNING: This action must be applied to clean systems. A BYOVD attack that has already succeeded (driver already loaded) cannot be stopped by blocklist policy — the driver is already running in the kernel. For hosts where BYOVD is suspected, prioritise host isolation (ID 4) and process termination (ID 74) before applying this control.

NOTE: SEE ALSO: ID 54 (Attack Surface Reduction Rules) includes the 'Block abuse of exploited vulnerable signed drivers' ASR rule, which complements this action by blocking driver-based privilege escalation attempts at the process layer. Run ID 54 and ID 76 together for layered BYOVD coverage.
